rules.rule_statement: Rule Absent language to the contrary, a lease can be freely assigned or sublet.
rules.short_rule_statement: Rule Absent language to the contrary, a lease can be freely assigned or sublet.
rules.source_text:
Assignment And Subletting
Rule Absent language to the contrary, a lease can be freely assigned or sublet.

rules.rule_statement: In most jurisdictions, a complete transfer of the tenant’s remaining lease term creates an assignment. A transfer for less than the entire duration of the lease creates a sublease. A transfer of a physical portion of leased property for the tenant’s remaining lease term creates a partial assignment. In some jurisdictions, the parties’ intent governs whether the transfer of a tenant’s interest creates an assignment or a sublease. In most jurisdictions, the transfer of a lease is subject to the Statute of Frauds if a lease of the same length as the assignment or sublease would be. Because a lease is both a contract and a conveyance, each can be an independent ground for liability.
rules.short_rule_statement: In most jurisdictions, a complete transfer of the tenant’s remaining lease term creates an assignment. A transfer for less than the entire duration of the lease creates a...
rules.source_text:
Assignment Versus Sublease
In most jurisdictions, a complete transfer of the tenant’s remaining lease term creates an assignment. A transfer for less than the entire duration of the lease creates a sublease. A transfer of a physical portion of leased property for the tenant’s remaining lease term creates a partial assignment. In some jurisdictions, the parties’ intent governs whether the transfer of a tenant’s interest creates an assignment or a sublease. In most jurisdictions, the transfer of a lease is subject to the Statute of Frauds if a lease of the same length as the assignment or sublease would be. Because a lease is both a contract and a conveyance, each can be an independent ground for liability.

rules.rule_statement: An assignee-tenant is in privity of estate with the landlord and, as such, is liable to the landlord for the rent and any other covenants in the lease that run with the lease. However, if the assignee-tenant reassigns the leasehold to a subsequent tenant, then the assignee-tenant’s privity of estate with the landlord ends. In that case, the initial assignee-tenant is no longer liable to the landlord based on privity of estate because the subsequent tenant is now in privity of estate with the landlord.
rules.short_rule_statement: An assignee-tenant is in privity of estate with the landlord and, as such, is liable to the landlord for the rent and any other covenants in the lease that run with the lease....
rules.source_text:
Assignee’S Rights And Liabilities
An assignee-tenant is in privity of estate with the landlord and, as such, is liable to the landlord for the rent and any other covenants in the lease that run with the lease. However, if the assignee-tenant reassigns the leasehold to a subsequent tenant, then the assignee-tenant’s privity of estate with the landlord ends. In that case, the initial assignee-tenant is no longer liable to the landlord based on privity of estate because the subsequent tenant is now in privity of estate with the landlord.

rules.rule_statement: Because the sublessee is not in privity of estate or contract with the landlord, the sublessee is not liable to the landlord for the rent or any other covenants in the lease but is liable to the lessee (sublessor). However, if the sublessee expressly assumes the rent covenant (or any other covenants), then the sublessee becomes personally liable to the landlord. Although the sublessee can enforce all covenants made by the original lessee (sublessor) in the sublease, the sublessee cannot enforce any covenants made by the landlord.
rules.short_rule_statement: Because the sublessee is not in privity of estate or contract with the landlord, the sublessee is not liable to the landlord for the rent or any other covenants in the lease...
rules.source_text:
Sublessee’S Rights And Liabilities
Because the sublessee is not in privity of estate or contract with the landlord, the sublessee is not liable to the landlord for the rent or any other covenants in the lease but is liable to the lessee (sublessor). However, if the sublessee expressly assumes the rent covenant (or any other covenants), then the sublessee becomes personally liable to the landlord. Although the sublessee can enforce all covenants made by the original lessee (sublessor) in the sublease, the sublessee cannot enforce any covenants made by the landlord.

rules.rule_statement: The privity of estate held by the original tenant terminates upon a successful assignment by the tenant to the assignee (but does not terminate upon a sublease). The original tenant remains in privity of contract with the landlord (both are parties to the lease agreement), so the original tenant remains liable for all the covenants in the lease for the duration of the lease, even after a successful assignment, unless the landlord relieves the original tenant of liability through a release or novation.
rules.short_rule_statement: The privity of estate held by the original tenant terminates upon a successful assignment by the tenant to the assignee (but does not terminate upon a sublease). The original...
rules.source_text:
Original Tenant’S Rights And Liabilities
The privity of estate held by the original tenant terminates upon a successful assignment by the tenant to the assignee (but does not terminate upon a sublease). The original tenant remains in privity of contract with the landlord (both are parties to the lease agreement), so the original tenant remains liable for all the covenants in the lease for the duration of the lease, even after a successful assignment, unless the landlord relieves the original tenant of liability through a release or novation.

rules.rule_statement: Unless the lease provides otherwise, a landlord may assign his rights and obligations under the lease, usually as part of a transfer of the landlord’s ownership interest in the property, to a third party without the tenant’s consent. The tenant owes rent, as well as any other burden imposed by a covenant in the lease that runs with the land, to the assignee-landlord. Likewise, the assignee-landlord is obligated to the tenant to perform any burden imposed by a covenant that runs with the land. The assignor landlord remains liable to the tenant for all covenants in the lease.
rules.short_rule_statement: Unless the lease provides otherwise, a landlord may assign his rights and obligations under the lease, usually as part of a transfer of the landlord’s ownership interest in the...
rules.source_text:
Landlord Assignments
Unless the lease provides otherwise, a landlord may assign his rights and obligations under the lease, usually as part of a transfer of the landlord’s ownership interest in the property, to a third party without the tenant’s consent. The tenant owes rent, as well as any other burden imposed by a covenant in the lease that runs with the land, to the assignee-landlord. Likewise, the assignee-landlord is obligated to the tenant to perform any burden imposed by a covenant that runs with the land. The assignor landlord remains liable to the tenant for all covenants in the lease.

rules.rule_statement: Attornment is the tenant’s acknowledgment of a new landlord. Although a tenant can do so formally in a writing, a tenant’s payment of rent to the new landlord is deemed an attornment. At common law, the tenant’s obligations under the lease did not come into existence until attornment. Today, a tenant’s obligations to an assignee-landlord arise automatically upon notice of the assignment, and attornment occurs primarily in the context of the transfer of ownership of leased commercial property. For example, a tenant in a commercial lease may be obligated under an attornment clause to acknowledge the tenant’s lease obligations to a person who gains ownership of the property through a foreclosure sale.
rules.short_rule_statement: Attornment is the tenant’s acknowledgment of a new landlord. Although a tenant can do so formally in a writing, a tenant’s payment of rent to the new landlord is deemed an...
rules.source_text:
Attornment
Attornment is the tenant’s acknowledgment of a new landlord. Although a tenant can do so formally in a writing, a tenant’s payment of rent to the new landlord is deemed an attornment. At common law, the tenant’s obligations under the lease did not come into existence until attornment. Today, a tenant’s obligations to an assignee-landlord arise automatically upon notice of the assignment, and attornment occurs primarily in the context of the transfer of ownership of leased commercial property. For example, a tenant in a commercial lease may be obligated under an attornment clause to acknowledge the tenant’s lease obligations to a person who gains ownership of the property through a foreclosure sale.

rules.rule_statement: When a lease prohibits the tenant from assignment or subletting the leasehold, the tenant may nevertheless assign or sublet the premises. However, the landlord generally can then terminate the lease for breach of one of its covenants and recover any damages.
rules.short_rule_statement: When a lease prohibits the tenant from assignment or subletting the leasehold, the tenant may nevertheless assign or sublet the premises. However, the landlord generally can...
rules.source_text:
Prohibition
When a lease prohibits the tenant from assignment or subletting the leasehold, the tenant may nevertheless assign or sublet the premises. However, the landlord generally can then terminate the lease for breach of one of its covenants and recover any damages.

rules.rule_statement: When a lease prevents assignment or subletting without the landlord’s permission and the lease is silent as to a standard for exercising that permission, the majority of jurisdictions impose a requirement that the landlord may withhold permission only on a reasonable ground in relationship to the property being leased and not on a whim or personal prejudice. The traditional rule is that the landlord may withhold permission at his discretion. Non-assignment and non-sublease clauses are valid but narrowly construed. A clause that prohibits assignment does not automatically also prohibit subleasing.
rules.short_rule_statement: When a lease prevents assignment or subletting without the landlord’s permission and the lease is silent as to a standard for exercising that permission, the majority of...
rules.source_text:
Landlord’S Permission
When a lease prevents assignment or subletting without the landlord’s permission and the lease is silent as to a standard for exercising that permission, the majority of jurisdictions impose a requirement that the landlord may withhold permission only on a reasonable ground in relationship to the property being leased and not on a whim or personal prejudice. The traditional rule is that the landlord may withhold permission at his discretion. Non-assignment and non-sublease clauses are valid but narrowly construed. A clause that prohibits assignment does not automatically also prohibit subleasing.

rules.rule_statement: The landlord’s right to object to an assignment or sublease may be waived if the landlord knows of either the assignment or sublease and does not object. When a landlord consents to an assignment or waives her right to object, she cannot then object to a subsequent assignment. This prohibition on an objection to a subsequent assignment does not apply to subsequent subleases, and a minority of jurisdictions do not impose such a prohibition even on a subsequent assignment.
rules.short_rule_statement: The landlord’s right to object to an assignment or sublease may be waived if the landlord knows of either the assignment or sublease and does not object. When a landlord...
rules.source_text:
Waiver By Landlord
The landlord’s right to object to an assignment or sublease may be waived if the landlord knows of either the assignment or sublease and does not object. When a landlord consents to an assignment or waives her right to object, she cannot then object to a subsequent assignment. This prohibition on an objection to a subsequent assignment does not apply to subsequent subleases, and a minority of jurisdictions do not impose such a prohibition even on a subsequent assignment.

rules.rule_statement: Condemnation is the taking of land for public use or because it is unfit for use. Tenant rights upon condemnation depend on whether the condemnation is partial or complete. If the condemnation is partial, meaning that only a portion of the leased property is condemned or the property is temporarily condemned, then the tenant must continue to make rent payments. The tenant is entitled to compensation for the portion of the property that was condemned or the time the tenant was dispossessed from the leased property. If the condemnation is complete, meaning that the entire leased property is taken for the balance of the lease term, then the tenant is discharged from the rent obligation and is entitled to compensation for the taking.
rules.short_rule_statement: Condemnation is the taking of land for public use or because it is unfit for use. Tenant rights upon condemnation depend on whether the condemnation is partial or complete. If...
rules.source_text:
Condemnation
Condemnation is the taking of land for public use or because it is unfit for use. Tenant rights upon condemnation depend on whether the condemnation is partial or complete. If the condemnation is partial, meaning that only a portion of the leased property is condemned or the property is temporarily condemned, then the tenant must continue to make rent payments. The tenant is entitled to compensation for the portion of the property that was condemned or the time the tenant was dispossessed from the leased property. If the condemnation is complete, meaning that the entire leased property is taken for the balance of the lease term, then the tenant is discharged from the rent obligation and is entitled to compensation for the taking.

rules.rule_statement: Rule A contract for the sale of real property (land-sales contract) is subject to the same formation requirements as any other contract. In other words, there must be an offer, acceptance, and consideration for the agreement to be enforceable. Because a contract for the sale of real property is subject to the Statute of Frauds, the contract generally must be in a signed writing and contain all of the essential terms of sale, including identification of the parties, property description, price, and terms of payment.
rules.short_rule_statement: Rule A contract for the sale of real property (land-sales contract) is subject to the same formation requirements as any other contract. In other words, there must be an offer,...
rules.source_text:
Formation
Rule A contract for the sale of real property (land-sales contract) is subject to the same formation requirements as any other contract. In other words, there must be an offer, acceptance, and consideration for the agreement to be enforceable. Because a contract for the sale of real property is subject to the Statute of Frauds, the contract generally must be in a signed writing and contain all of the essential terms of sale, including identification of the parties, property description, price, and terms of payment.

rules.rule_statement: In many instances, the sale of real property involves a real estate broker. The rights and duties of the broker are generally governed by contract, although state law requires a person to be licensed to serve as a broker and often imposes various requirements. All states also recognize and license real estate salespersons (typically referred to as “real estate agents”) who generally serve as a subagent of a real estate broker. Generally, when a person who is required to be licensed fails to obtain a license, the person is not entitled to a commission on the sale of the real property.
rules.short_rule_statement: In many instances, the sale of real property involves a real estate broker. The rights and duties of the broker are generally governed by contract, although state law requires...
rules.source_text:
Real Estate Broker
In many instances, the sale of real property involves a real estate broker. The rights and duties of the broker are generally governed by contract, although state law requires a person to be licensed to serve as a broker and often imposes various requirements. All states also recognize and license real estate salespersons (typically referred to as “real estate agents”) who generally serve as a subagent of a real estate broker. Generally, when a person who is required to be licensed fails to obtain a license, the person is not entitled to a commission on the sale of the real property.

rules.rule_statement: In general, a real estate broker, as an agent, owes the principal the duties that any agent owes to a principal, including the fiduciary duties of loyalty, care, competence, diligence, disclosure, and confidentiality, subject to the modification of those duties by an agreement between the seller and the broker. For example, a broker has a duty to disclose any interest that a related person or entity (e.g., family member, controlled corporation) has in the sale or purchase of the property. Typically, a real estate broker serves as the seller’s agent. There is usually a listing broker, who deals directly with the seller and helps to set the asking price. The listing broker typically assumes primary responsibilities for advertising the property for sale. There is usually also a selling broker, who serves as a subagent of the listing broker and finds a buyer for the property. Generally, the listing broker and the selling broker share in the commission paid by the seller. More recently, a real estate broker may serve as the buyer’s agent. Typically, such an agent also receives a commission- based fee in an amount similar to the selling agent’s. A dual agent serves as agent for both the buyer and the seller of real property. This situation may arise when the real estate agents who represent the buyer and the seller of a residence are themselves each agents of the same real estate broker. It may also arise when a real estate broker represents a seller in the sale of the seller’s residence and the buyer of that transaction in the sale of the buyer’s residence, which typically is a condition of the buyer’s purchase of the seller’s residence. Many states prohibit a person from serving as a dual agent because of the possibility of a conflict of interest. In states that permit a person to serve as a dual agent, the person is required to disclose the dual agency to the buyer and the seller and obtain the informed consent of each. Many states recognize a broker who does not serve as an agent of either the buyer or the seller but instead offers to facilitate the sale by performing various functions, such as finding a lender who is willing to finance the transaction. This type of broker, who is generally referred to as a “transaction broker,” owes each party the duty to perform the broker’s tasks with the necessary skill, care, and diligence and to deal fairly with each party but does not owe either party a duty of loyalty. When a real estate broker facilitates the leasing of real property (i.e., leasing broker) as an agent for a party, the broker typically is acting as an agent for the landlord. When a real estate broker facilitates the acquisition of a mortgage loan (i.e., mortgage broker) for a party, the broker is typically the agent of the borrower.

rules.source_text:
Types Of Agents
In general, a real estate broker, as an agent, owes the principal the duties that any agent owes to a principal, including the fiduciary duties of loyalty, care, competence, diligence, disclosure, and confidentiality, subject to the modification of those duties by an agreement between the seller and the broker. For example, a broker has a duty to disclose any interest that a related person or entity (e.g., family member, controlled corporation) has in the sale or purchase of the property. Typically, a real estate broker serves as the seller’s agent. There is usually a listing broker, who deals directly with the seller and helps to set the asking price. The listing broker typically assumes primary responsibilities for advertising the property for sale. There is usually also a selling broker, who serves as a subagent of the listing broker and finds a buyer for the property. Generally, the listing broker and the selling broker share in the commission paid by the seller. Mo

rules.rule_statement: If the agreement provides that the broker has a nonexclusive agency or if the listing agreement between the seller of real property and the real estate broker does not address requirements for the broker to earn a commission, the broker’s efforts must be the procuring cause for the buyer’s purchase of the property for the broker to be entitled to a commission (i.e., the resulting sale must be induced primarily by those efforts).
rules.short_rule_statement: If the agreement provides that the broker has a nonexclusive agency or if the listing agreement between the seller of real property and the real estate broker does not address...
rules.source_text:
Nonexclusive Agency
If the agreement provides that the broker has a nonexclusive agency or if the listing agreement between the seller of real property and the real estate broker does not address requirements for the broker to earn a commission, the broker’s efforts must be the procuring cause for the buyer’s purchase of the property for the broker to be entitled to a commission (i.e., the resulting sale must be induced primarily by those efforts).

rules.rule_statement: If the agreement provides that the broker has an exclusive agency, then the broker is entitled to a commission if her effort (or that of any other broker) is the procuring cause for the buyer’s purchase of the property.
rules.short_rule_statement: If the agreement provides that the broker has an exclusive agency, then the broker is entitled to a commission if her effort (or that of any other broker) is the procuring...
rules.source_text:
Exclusive Agency
If the agreement provides that the broker has an exclusive agency, then the broker is entitled to a commission if her effort (or that of any other broker) is the procuring cause for the buyer’s purchase of the property.

rules.rule_statement: If the agreement provides that the broker has an exclusive right to sell the property, then the broker is entitled to a commission if the efforts of anyone, even the seller, are the procuring cause for the buyer’s purchase of the property. Most listing agreements provide for payment of a commission to the real estate broker upon the procurement of a buyer who is ready, willing, and able to purchase the property on terms that are agreeable to the seller. Consequently, a broker generally has a right to a commission when the contract of sale is executed or, if the contract is subject to conditions, when the conditions are satisfied, even if the sale does not occur. However, in a minority of states, the broker is not entitled to a commission if the buyer defaults (“no sale, no commission” rule). However, a listing agreement may provide the broker with a share of any recovery gained by the seller from the defaulting buyer (e.g., retention of a deposit). Under the equal-dignities rule, many states require a contract to pay a commission to a real estate agent or broker to be in writing because the underlying contract to sell the real property must be in writing.
rules.short_rule_statement: If the agreement provides that the broker has an exclusive right to sell the property, then the broker is entitled to a commission if the efforts of anyone, even the seller,...
rules.source_text:
Exclusive Right To Sell
If the agreement provides that the broker has an exclusive right to sell the property, then the broker is entitled to a commission if the efforts of anyone, even the seller, are the procuring cause for the buyer’s purchase of the property. Most listing agreements provide for payment of a commission to the real estate broker upon the procurement of a buyer who is ready, willing, and able to purchase the property on terms that are agreeable to the seller. Consequently, a broker generally has a right to a commission when the contract of sale is executed or, if the contract is subject to conditions, when the conditions are satisfied, even if the sale does not occur. However, in a minority of states, the broker is not entitled to a commission if the buyer defaults (“no sale, no commission” rule). However, a listing agreement may provide the broker with a share of any recovery gained by the seller from the defaulting buyer (e.g., retention of a deposit). Under the equ

rules.rule_statement: Most states recognize that a real estate agent or broker who is not an attorney may prepare a contract for the sale of real property. Many states limit the types of contracts that a real estate agent or broker may prepare to standard-form contracts that can usually be completed by filling in blanks in the form. In some states, the focus is on whether the agent or broker charges a separate fee for preparing the contract and whether it is incident to the agent’s or broker’s regular course of business. In other states, the real estate agent or broker is required to provide a written notice or require that the contract itself contain a contingency regarding attorney review or approval.
rules.short_rule_statement: Most states recognize that a real estate agent or broker who is not an attorney may prepare a contract for the sale of real property. Many states limit the types of contracts...
rules.source_text:
Preparation Of Contract Of Sale
Most states recognize that a real estate agent or broker who is not an attorney may prepare a contract for the sale of real property. Many states limit the types of contracts that a real estate agent or broker may prepare to standard-form contracts that can usually be completed by filling in blanks in the form. In some states, the focus is on whether the agent or broker charges a separate fee for preparing the contract and whether it is incident to the agent’s or broker’s regular course of business. In other states, the real estate agent or broker is required to provide a written notice or require that the contract itself contain a contingency regarding attorney review or approval.

rules.rule_statement: Rule A land-sales contract must:
rules.short_rule_statement: Rule A land-sales contract must:
rules.source_text:
Statute Of Frauds
Rule A land-sales contract must:

rules.rule_statement: ii) Be signed by the party to be charged; and iii) Contain all of the essential terms: parties, property description, and terms of price and payment. Other real property instruments: The Statute of Frauds also applies to a promise to create an interest in real property, the assignment of a right to purchase real property, an option contract for the sale of an interest in real property, and a promise to give a mortgage or other lien as security. Although leases, both creating property interests and serving as contracts, are generally subject to the Statute of Frauds, a lease for one year or less is usually not subject to the statute. In addition, licenses are not within the statute. Note that a deed (i.e., a document that conveys a real property interest) must be in writing, signed by the grantor, contain a description of the property, and identify the grantee; in addition, the deed must contain words of transfer. (See § IV.B.2.b. Contents of the Deed, infra, regarding the requirements for a valid deed.)
rules.short_rule_statement: ii) Be signed by the party to be charged; and iii) Contain all of the essential terms: parties, property description, and terms of price and payment. Other real property...
rules.source_text:
Be In Writing
ii) Be signed by the party to be charged; and iii) Contain all of the essential terms: parties, property description, and terms of price and payment. Other real property instruments: The Statute of Frauds also applies to a promise to create an interest in real property, the assignment of a right to purchase real property, an option contract for the sale of an interest in real property, and a promise to give a mortgage or other lien as security. Although leases, both creating property interests and serving as contracts, are generally subject to the Statute of Frauds, a lease for one year or less is usually not subject to the statute. In addition, licenses are not within the statute. Note that a deed (i.e., a document that conveys a real property interest) must be in writing, signed by the grantor, contain a description of the property, and identify the grantee; in addition, the deed must contain words of transfer. (See § IV.B.2.b. Contents of the Deed, infra, regarding the

rules.rule_statement: When a party to an oral contract who has promised to convey real property performs, that party can enforce the other party’s oral promise unless the promise is itself the transfer of a real property interest. Example: During a face-to-face conversation, a seller agrees to transfer land to a buyer in exchange for the buyer’s promise to pay $50,000 to the seller. The seller tenders the deed to the buyer, and the buyer accepts the deed. The seller can enforce the buyer’s oral promise to pay $50,000 to the seller.

Full Performance
When a party to an oral contract who has promised to convey real property performs, that party can enforce the other party’s oral promise unless the promise is itself the transfer of a real property interest. Example: During a face-to-face conversation, a seller agrees to transfer land to a buyer in exchange for the buyer’s promise to pay $50,000 to the seller. The seller tenders the deed to the buyer, and the buyer accepts the deed. The seller can enforce the buyer’s oral promise to pay $50,000 to the seller.

rules.rule_statement: Specific performance may also be permitted when the party seeking enforcement has reasonably relied on the contract and would suffer such hardship that the other party will be estopped from asserting the Statute of Frauds as a defense to the contract.

rules.rule_statement: Although a writing is generally required to create an enforceable land-sales contract, most states permit the oral rescission (i.e., cancellation) of a land-sales contract. Restatement (Third) of Agency § 3.02, cmt. e. Commonwealth v. Jones & Robins, Inc., 41 S.E.2d 720 (Va. 1947) (preparation of simple contract of sale permitted); 4 Colo. Code Regs. § 725-1 E-4,33. In re Op. No. 26 of the Comm., 654 A.2d 1344 (N.J. 1995) (notice must spell out who the broker represents and give the parties three days in which to seek the advice of an attorney and to cancel the contract); Duncan & Hill Realty v. Dep’t of State, 405 N.Y.S.2d 339 (App. Div. 1978); Opinion of N.Y.S. Attorney General 96-F11, dated November 14, 1996 (the contract must contain a clause that the contract is subject to the approval of the parties’ attorneys).

rules.rule_statement: Absent contrary language, an implied covenant of marketable title is part of a land-sales contract, regardless of the type of deed created. Marketable title is a title that is free from defects that create an unreasonable risk of litigation. The covenant of marketable title does not require that the property be free of all defects.  Rather, it requires disclosure of existing defects such that a potential buyer can make an informed decision about purchasing the property.  So, in effect, it is undisclosed defects that may render title unmarketable.

Absent contrary language, an implied covenant of marketable title is part of a land-sales contract, regardless of the type of deed created. The covenant of marketable title does not require that the property be free of all defects.  Rather, it requires disclosure of existing defects such that a potential buyer can make an informed decision about purchasing the property.  So, in effect, it is undisclosed defects that may render title unmarketable.

rules.rule_statement: Unless otherwise agreed, the seller is not required to deliver marketable title until the closing (i.e., termination of the real estate sale, when ownership transfers and the deed is delivered to the buyer). Regarding an outstanding mortgage, the seller is permitted to apply the proceeds from the sale of the property to the mortgage obligation. If the sale proceeds meet or exceed the amount of the outstanding mortgage, the seller, by doing so, can eliminate this title defect. In the case of an installment land contract, marketable title is not required to be given until delivery occurs.

Timing
Unless otherwise agreed, the seller is not required to deliver marketable title until the closing (i.e., termination of the real estate sale, when ownership transfers and the deed is delivered to the buyer). Regarding an outstanding mortgage, the seller is permitted to apply the proceeds from the sale of the property to the mortgage obligation. If the sale proceeds meet or exceed the amount of the outstanding mortgage, the seller, by doing so, can eliminate this title defect. In the case of an installment land contract, marketable title is not required to be given until delivery occurs.

rules.rule_statement: Generally, a court will assume that time is not of the essence in a real estate contract unless:
rules.short_rule_statement: Generally, a court will assume that time is not of the essence in a real estate contract unless:
rules.source_text:
Time Of The Essence
Generally, a court will assume that time is not of the essence in a real estate contract unless:

rules.canonical_name: The Contract Specifically States That Time Is Of The Essence
rules.rule_statement: ii) Circumstances indicate that this was the parties’ intention; or iii) One party gives the other party notice that time is of the essence. Such notice must be given at a reasonable time before the date designated for closing. If time is not of the essence, strict adherence to the closing date set in the contract will not be required in equity. Thus, a failure to perform will generally not be grounds for rescission of the contract. A party can sue for specific performance, though, as long as the party was ready to perform within a reasonable time from the date set for performance. Regardless of whether time is of the essence in a real estate contract, the party that fails to render performance on the date set for closing in the contract will be in breach and liable for damages in an action at law for incidental losses such as taxes, interest, etc.

Such notice must be given at a reasonable time before the date designated for closing. If time is not of the essence, strict adherence to the closing date set in the contract will not be required in equity. Thus, a failure to perform will generally not be grounds for rescission of the contract. A party can sue for specific performance, though, as long as the party was ready to perform within a reasonable time from the date set for performance. Regardless of whether time is of the essence in a real estate contract, the party that fails to render performance on the date set for closing in the contract will be in breach and liable for damages in an action at law for incidental losses such as taxes, interest, etc.

rules.canonical_name: Implied Warranty Of Fitness Or Suitability (New Homes)
rules.rule_statement: A warranty of fitness or suitability (or a warranty of quality, workmanlike construction, performance, or habitability) is implied in almost all jurisdictions in a contract for the sale of a newly constructed residence. In some jurisdictions, this warranty extends to the repair or remodeling of an existing residence and even to related structures (e.g., a detached garage, a retaining wall). Under this warranty, the seller generally warrants that a new home was built according to certain standards of workmanship and that it is suitable for living. Historical Note: Caveat Emptor The implied warranty of fitness or suitability replaces the common-law doctrine of caveat emptor (“let the buyer beware”), which put the burden on the buyer to reasonably inspect the property before purchasing it. Some states follow one of the below approaches, while other states recognize both prongs of this warranty:

Implied Warranty Of Fitness Or Suitability (New Homes)

Under this warranty, the seller generally warrants that a new home was built according to certain standards of workmanship and that it is suitable for living. Some states follow one of the below approaches, while other states recognize both prongs of this warranty:

rules.rule_statement: In some states, the warranty parallels the warranty of merchantability given by a merchant of goods under Article 2 of the Uniform Commercial Code. Under this type of warranty, the seller warrants that adequate materials were used and good workmanship was used on the residence. The implied warranty generally covers latent construction defects (i.e., defects that cannot be discovered by reasonable observation or inspection), such as a defective electrical, plumbing, or mechanical system, or a leaky roof or drainage problem that does not manifest until after the sale. Although the buyer has a duty to conduct a reasonable inspection of the residence for patent defects, the buyer is not required to employ an expert home inspector.

Approach—Good Workmanship
In some states, the warranty parallels the warranty of merchantability given by a merchant of goods under Article 2 of the Uniform Commercial Code. Under this type of warranty, the seller warrants that adequate materials were used and good workmanship was used on the residence. The implied warranty generally covers latent construction defects (i.e., defects that cannot be discovered by reasonable observation or inspection), such as a defective electrical, plumbing, or mechanical system, or a leaky roof or drainage problem that does not manifest until after the sale. Although the buyer has a duty to conduct a reasonable inspection of the residence for patent defects, the buyer is not required to employ an expert home inspector.

rules.rule_statement: In other states, the warranty parallels the warranty of habitability given by a landlord to a residential tenant. Under this type of warranty, the focus is on whether the defect significantly affects the buyer’s ability to live in the residence.

rules.rule_statement: Generally, a suit for breach of this warranty must be brought within a reasonable time. When the warranty is statutory, a fixed time is generally specified (e.g., one year, 10 years) and may vary with the type of defect (e.g., structural defects, foundation problems). Many states apply both: defect; and 2) A statute of repose that typically begins to run with the completion of the residence or the initial buyer’s possession of it. Example: A state sets a three-year statute of limitations for claims brought under a tort or contract theory. The state also has a six-year statute of repose that begins to run upon completion of the home. A buyer purchased a house the same year that construction was completed (starting the statute of repose). Four years later, the buyer discovers a defect (starting the statute of limitations). The buyer now has three years to bring the claim under the statute of limitations and three years to bring the claim under the statute of repose. The buyer must bring the claim within the two years that remain under the statute of repose, rather than the longer three-year period for bringing tort or contract claims. Some states provide a short extension of the time in which to bring a claim if the defect is discovered toward the end of the statute of repose.

rules.rule_statement: This implied warranty may be disclaimed by the builder or waived by the homeowner if done so with language that is clear and unambiguous, but a general disclaimer (e.g., “Property is sold as is”) is typically not sufficient. Some jurisdictions require the homeowner to give the builder notice of the defect and an opportunity to correct it before the homeowner can sue.

rules.source_text:
Disclaimer, Waiver, And Notice
This implied warranty may be disclaimed by the builder or waived by the homeowner if done so with language that is clear and unambiguous, but a general disclaimer (e.g., “Property is sold as is”) is typically not sufficient. Some jurisdictions require the homeowner to give the builder notice of the defect and an opportunity to correct it before the homeowner can sue.

rules.rule_statement: In a majority of jurisdictions, a seller of a residence has a duty to disclose all known material physical defects to the buyer. The defect must not be readily observable or known to the buyer. To be material, the defect must substantially affect the value of the residence, impact the health or safety of a resident (an objective standard), or affect the desirability of the residence to the buyer (a subjective standard). When a seller fails to make such disclosures, the buyer may rescind the sale contract or seek damages. Some states limit this duty to commercial sellers (e.g., builders); other states impose this duty on all sellers. Even in a jurisdiction that does not require an affirmative disclosure by the seller, a buyer can sue the seller for misrepresentation or fraudulent concealment. General disclaimers (e.g., “Property is sold as is”) are not sufficient to preclude the seller’s liability for misrepresentation or fraudulent concealment.

rules.source_text:
Duty To Disclose Defects (All Homes)
In a majority of jurisdictions, a seller of a residence has a duty to disclose all known material physical defects to the buyer. The defect must not be readily observable or known to the buyer. To be material, the defect must substantially affect the value of the residence, impact the health or safety of a resident (an objective standard), or affect the desirability of the residence to the buyer (a subjective standard). When a seller fails to make such disclosures, the buyer may rescind the sale contract or seek damages. Some states limit this duty to commercial sellers (e.g., builders); other states impose this duty on all sellers. Even in a jurisdiction that does not require an affirmative disclosure by the seller, a buyer can sue the seller for misrepresentation or fraudulent concealment. General disclaimers (e.g., “Property is sold as is”) are not sufficient to preclude the seller’s liability for misrepresentation or fraudulent concealment.

rules.rule_statement: The seller’s and buyer’s performances are concurrent conditions. Therefore, one is not obligated to perform without the other’s performance. When one party, either the buyer or the seller, repudiates the contract, the nonrepudiating party is excused from performing. However, if both parties fail to perform, then the closing date is usually extended until one of the parties performs or repudiates the contract. The seller’s inability to produce a marketable title does not automatically create a material breach; rather, the buyer must give the seller sufficient time to cure the title defect unless time is of the essence and the defect cannot be cured before the time fixed for closing.
rules.short_rule_statement: The seller’s and buyer’s performances are concurrent conditions. Therefore, one is not obligated to perform without the other’s performance. When one party, either the buyer or...
rules.source_text:
Tender Of Performance
The seller’s and buyer’s performances are concurrent conditions. Therefore, one is not obligated to perform without the other’s performance. When one party, either the buyer or the seller, repudiates the contract, the nonrepudiating party is excused from performing. However, if both parties fail to perform, then the closing date is usually extended until one of the parties performs or repudiates the contract. The seller’s inability to produce a marketable title does not automatically create a material breach; rather, the buyer must give the seller sufficient time to cure the title defect unless time is of the essence and the defect cannot be cured before the time fixed for closing.

rules.rule_statement: Under the common-law doctrine of merger, an obligation contained in the contract of sale, such as the seller’s duty to deliver marketable title or a description of the size or location of the property to be transferred, is merged into the deed and cannot thereafter be enforced unless the deed contains the obligation. If a buyer wants to enforce an obligation in the contract, the buyer must do so at or before closing. After the closing, the seller has completely performed the contract, and therefore, the buyer can no longer bring a breach of contract claim related to those obligations. But the buyer may bring a claim under the deed (if the deed includes the obligation). If the deed does not include the obligation, it is no longer actionable.
rules.short_rule_statement: Under the common-law doctrine of merger, an obligation contained in the contract of sale, such as the seller’s duty to deliver marketable title or a description of the size or...
rules.source_text:
Merger
Under the common-law doctrine of merger, an obligation contained in the contract of sale, such as the seller’s duty to deliver marketable title or a description of the size or location of the property to be transferred, is merged into the deed and cannot thereafter be enforced unless the deed contains the obligation. If a buyer wants to enforce an obligation in the contract, the buyer must do so at or before closing. After the closing, the seller has completely performed the contract, and therefore, the buyer can no longer bring a breach of contract claim related to those obligations. But the buyer may bring a claim under the deed (if the deed includes the obligation). If the deed does not include the obligation, it is no longer actionable.

rules.rule_statement: The doctrine of merger is subject to exceptions, including the parties’ intent, either expressed or implied, that the obligation survive the deed, such as an obligation that cannot be performed until after ownership of the property has passed to the buyer.
rules.short_rule_statement: The doctrine of merger is subject to exceptions, including the parties’ intent, either expressed or implied, that the obligation survive the deed, such as an obligation that...
rules.source_text:
Exceptions
The doctrine of merger is subject to exceptions, including the parties’ intent, either expressed or implied, that the obligation survive the deed, such as an obligation that cannot be performed until after ownership of the property has passed to the buyer.

rules.rule_statement: In addition, the doctrine generally is not applicable to an obligation that is collateral to and independent of the conveyance itself (e.g., an agreement to reconvey, in the future, a portion of the property presently conveyed). The merger doctrine does not prevent a buyer from raising other contract defenses, such as fraud or mutual mistake. Note that the term “merger” is used in other real property contexts, such as the merger of property interests (e.g., acquisition of both the land that is subject to and the land that is benefited by a restrictive covenant). Such a merger results in the extinguishment of the property right (e.g., the restrictive covenant).

Inapplicability
In addition, the doctrine generally is not applicable to an obligation that is collateral to and independent of the conveyance itself (e.g., an agreement to reconvey, in the future, a portion of the property presently conveyed). The merger doctrine does not prevent a buyer from raising other contract defenses, such as fraud or mutual mistake. Note that the term “merger” is used in other real property contexts, such as the merger of property interests (e.g., acquisition of both the land that is subject to and the land that is benefited by a restrictive covenant). Such a merger results in the extinguishment of the property right (e.g., the restrictive covenant).

In addition, a buyer or seller can recover incidental damages, such as expenses associated with a title search or survey of the property.

rules.source_text:
Limitations On Damages
In some jurisdictions (about half), if the seller’s breach resulted from an inability to deliver marketable title but the seller acted in good faith, then the buyer’s damages are limited to the buyer’s out-of-pocket expenses. In addition, a buyer or seller can recover incidental damages, such as expenses associated with a title search or survey of the property.

rules.rule_statement: A real property contract usually requires the buyer to make a deposit of a portion of the purchase price (often referred to as “earnest money”). In addition, the contract typically contains a liquidated-damages clause that provides for the seller to retain the buyer’s deposit if the buyer breaches the contract and refuses to purchase the property. Rule In general, for a liquidated-damages clause to be enforceable, the amount of liquidated damages must be reasonable. Traditionally, many courts have found a deposit of no more than 10% of the purchase price to be reasonable as a liquidated- damages amount. However, courts may consider factors such as the sophistication of the buyer and the nature of the transaction (commercial versus residential) to determine the reasonableness of the seller’s retention of the buyer’s deposit. In addition, a court may refuse to enforce a liquidated-damages clause when the seller suffers no actual loss. When the contract is silent as to dispensation of the deposit in the event of a default by the buyer, courts consider the same factors and usually allow the seller to retain the deposit when reasonable.
rules.short_rule_statement: A real property contract usually requires the buyer to make a deposit of a portion of the purchase price (often referred to as “earnest money”). In addition, the contract...
rules.source_text:
Buyer’S Deposit As Liquidated Damages
A real property contract usually requires the buyer to make a deposit of a portion of the purchase price (often referred to as “earnest money”). In addition, the contract typically contains a liquidated-damages clause that provides for the seller to retain the buyer’s deposit if the buyer breaches the contract and refuses to purchase the property. Rule In general, for a liquidated-damages clause to be enforceable, the amount of liquidated damages must be reasonable. Traditionally, many courts have found a deposit of no more than 10% of the purchase price to be reasonable as a liquidated- damages amount. However, courts may consider factors such as the sophistication of the buyer and the nature of the transaction (commercial versus residential) to determine the reasonableness of the seller’s retention of the buyer’s deposit. In addition, a court may refuse to enforce a liquidated-damages clause when the seller suffers no actual loss. When the contra

rules.rule_statement: The buyer is entitled to specific performance for a seller’s breach of a contract to sell a real property interest because the buyer’s remedy at law is considered inadequate, given the unique nature of land. Under the theory of mutuality of remedies, the seller is also permitted to seek specific performance and force the buyer to purchase a real property interest, although some courts have rejected this theory because the seller typically receives money rather than real property.
rules.short_rule_statement: The buyer is entitled to specific performance for a seller’s breach of a contract to sell a real property interest because the buyer’s remedy at law is considered inadequate,...
rules.source_text:
Speciﬁc Performance
The buyer is entitled to specific performance for a seller’s breach of a contract to sell a real property interest because the buyer’s remedy at law is considered inadequate, given the unique nature of land. Under the theory of mutuality of remedies, the seller is also permitted to seek specific performance and force the buyer to purchase a real property interest, although some courts have rejected this theory because the seller typically receives money rather than real property.

rules.rule_statement: When the buyer seeks specific performance with respect to property for which there is a title defect (e.g., an encumbrance), the buyer may also obtain an abatement in the purchase price to compensate the buyer for the defect.
rules.short_rule_statement: When the buyer seeks specific performance with respect to property for which there is a title defect (e.g., an encumbrance), the buyer may also obtain an abatement in the...
rules.source_text:
Abatement For Defect
When the buyer seeks specific performance with respect to property for which there is a title defect (e.g., an encumbrance), the buyer may also obtain an abatement in the purchase price to compensate the buyer for the defect.

rules.rule_statement: The process of selling real property often takes weeks or months to complete, from the beginning of negotiations to transfer of the deed at closing. During that period, issues may arise as to who owns what interest in the property. The doctrine of equitable conversion provides some guidance on those issues. Rule Under the doctrine of equitable conversion, the seller retains legal title to real property during the pendency of the sales contract, and equitable title passes to the buyer upon entering the contract. The seller effectively holds the property in trust for the buyer and has a duty to keep up the property. However, as the holder of legal title, the seller has the right to possess the property. Equitable conversion does not apply if the contract is not specifically enforceable.
rules.short_rule_statement: The process of selling real property often takes weeks or months to complete, from the beginning of negotiations to transfer of the deed at closing. During that period, issues...
rules.source_text:
Equitable Conversion
The process of selling real property often takes weeks or months to complete, from the beginning of negotiations to transfer of the deed at closing. During that period, issues may arise as to who owns what interest in the property. The doctrine of equitable conversion provides some guidance on those issues. Rule Under the doctrine of equitable conversion, the seller retains legal title to real property during the pendency of the sales contract, and equitable title passes to the buyer upon entering the contract. The seller effectively holds the property in trust for the buyer and has a duty to keep up the property. However, as the holder of legal title, the seller has the right to possess the property. Equitable conversion does not apply if the contract is not specifically enforceable.

rules.rule_statement: When an action is maintained against the seller for a claim that arose before the execution of the contract, a judgment obtained against the seller after the execution of the contract is not enforceable against the real property. Under the doctrine of equitable conversion, the seller’s interest is converted by the contract into an interest in the proceeds from the sale; it is no longer an interest in the real property itself.
rules.short_rule_statement: When an action is maintained against the seller for a claim that arose before the execution of the contract, a judgment obtained against the seller after the execution of the...
rules.source_text:
Action Against The Seller
When an action is maintained against the seller for a claim that arose before the execution of the contract, a judgment obtained against the seller after the execution of the contract is not enforceable against the real property. Under the doctrine of equitable conversion, the seller’s interest is converted by the contract into an interest in the proceeds from the sale; it is no longer an interest in the real property itself.

rules.rule_statement: Most states, following the logic of the doctrine of equitable conversion, place the risk of loss during the time between the execution of the contract and the closing on the buyer, regardless of whether the buyer takes possession of the property. There is an exception when the loss is attributable to the seller’s intentional or negligent actions. The Uniform Vendor and Purchaser Risk Act (adopted by a minority of jurisdictions) keeps the risk of loss with the seller unless and until the buyer takes possession or title is transferred. For this act to apply, a material part of the property must be destroyed.
rules.short_rule_statement: Most states, following the logic of the doctrine of equitable conversion, place the risk of loss during the time between the execution of the contract and the closing on the...
rules.source_text:
Risk Of Loss
Most states, following the logic of the doctrine of equitable conversion, place the risk of loss during the time between the execution of the contract and the closing on the buyer, regardless of whether the buyer takes possession of the property. There is an exception when the loss is attributable to the seller’s intentional or negligent actions. The Uniform Vendor and Purchaser Risk Act (adopted by a minority of jurisdictions) keeps the risk of loss with the seller unless and until the buyer takes possession or title is transferred. For this act to apply, a material part of the property must be destroyed.

rules.rule_statement: Unless the contract requires otherwise, the seller does not have a duty to carry casualty insurance. Because the buyer has an equitable interest in the property, the buyer may obtain such insurance. When the risk of loss is on the buyer and the seller has casualty insurance, the seller is generally required to give the buyer credit against the purchase price in the amount of the insurance proceeds when a casualty occurs.
rules.short_rule_statement: Unless the contract requires otherwise, the seller does not have a duty to carry casualty insurance. Because the buyer has an equitable interest in the property, the buyer may...
rules.source_text:
Insurance
Unless the contract requires otherwise, the seller does not have a duty to carry casualty insurance. Because the buyer has an equitable interest in the property, the buyer may obtain such insurance. When the risk of loss is on the buyer and the seller has casualty insurance, the seller is generally required to give the buyer credit against the purchase price in the amount of the insurance proceeds when a casualty occurs.

rules.canonical_name: Effect Of The Buyer’S Or Seller’S Death
rules.rule_statement: When one of the contracting parties dies before the performance date of the contract, the seller’s interest may be treated as personal property, and the buyer’s interest may be treated as a real property interest to distribute the property pursuant to either’s will.
rules.short_rule_statement: When one of the contracting parties dies before the performance date of the contract, the seller’s interest may be treated as personal property, and the buyer’s interest may be...
rules.source_text:
Effect Of The Buyer’S Or Seller’S Death
When one of the contracting parties dies before the performance date of the contract, the seller’s interest may be treated as personal property, and the buyer’s interest may be treated as a real property interest to distribute the property pursuant to either’s will.
topic_path: Effect Of The Buyer’S Or Seller’S Death

rules.rule_statement: When the seller-decedent has devised his real property interests, the proceeds from the sale of the property under contract are treated as personal property that passes to the devisee of the seller-decedent’s personal property. The devise of the real property itself is treated as having been adeemed. In jurisdictions that have adopted an anti-ademption statute, the devisee of the seller?decedent’s real property is entitled to the sale proceeds. Crossover Topic: Wills For a discussion of ademption, see the Themis Wills outline.
rules.short_rule_statement: When the seller-decedent has devised his real property interests, the proceeds from the sale of the property under contract are treated as personal property that passes to the...
rules.source_text:
Seller’S Death
When the seller-decedent has devised his real property interests, the proceeds from the sale of the property under contract are treated as personal property that passes to the devisee of the seller-decedent’s personal property. The devise of the real property itself is treated as having been adeemed. In jurisdictions that have adopted an anti-ademption statute, the devisee of the seller?decedent’s real property is entitled to the sale proceeds. Crossover Topic: Wills For a discussion of ademption, see the Themis Wills outline.

rules.rule_statement: The person entitled to the buyer-decedent’s real property, such as the devisee of the buyer-decedent’s real property pursuant to the buyer?decedent’s will, can compel the transfer of the property to herself.
rules.short_rule_statement: The person entitled to the buyer-decedent’s real property, such as the devisee of the buyer-decedent’s real property pursuant to the buyer?decedent’s will, can compel the...
rules.source_text:
Buyer’S Death
The person entitled to the buyer-decedent’s real property, such as the devisee of the buyer-decedent’s real property pursuant to the buyer?decedent’s will, can compel the transfer of the property to herself.

rules.canonical_name: Options And Rights Of First Refusal
rules.rule_statement: An option to purchase real property (or a right of first refusal regarding the transfer of property) involves special considerations. As an interest in property, each is subject to the Statute of Frauds and the applicable recording act.
rules.short_rule_statement: An option to purchase real property (or a right of first refusal regarding the transfer of property) involves special considerations. As an interest in property, each is...
rules.source_text:
Options And Rights Of First Refusal
An option to purchase real property (or a right of first refusal regarding the transfer of property) involves special considerations. As an interest in property, each is subject to the Statute of Frauds and the applicable recording act.
topic_path: Options And Rights Of First Refusal

rules.rule_statement: In an option contract, one party acquires the right to purchase property, typically during a specific period, in exchange for consideration.
rules.short_rule_statement: In an option contract, one party acquires the right to purchase property, typically during a specific period, in exchange for consideration.
rules.source_text:
Option Contract
In an option contract, one party acquires the right to purchase property, typically during a specific period, in exchange for consideration.
topic_path: Options And Rights Of First Refusal

rules.rule_statement: An option contract removes a grantor’s ability to revoke the offer to sell. Otherwise, an offeror is free to withdraw the offer, despite a promise to the contrary, unless the offeree has detrimentally relied on the offer. In addition, as a contract, the option does not terminate upon the death or incapacity of the grantor of the option, unlike the termination of an offer upon the offeror’s death.
rules.short_rule_statement: An option contract removes a grantor’s ability to revoke the offer to sell. Otherwise, an offeror is free to withdraw the offer, despite a promise to the contrary, unless the...
rules.source_text:
Protection Against Revocation And Termination
An option contract removes a grantor’s ability to revoke the offer to sell. Otherwise, an offeror is free to withdraw the offer, despite a promise to the contrary, unless the offeree has detrimentally relied on the offer. In addition, as a contract, the option does not terminate upon the death or incapacity of the grantor of the option, unlike the termination of an offer upon the offeror’s death.
topic_path: Options And Rights Of First Refusal

rules.rule_statement: The holder of an option can make an offer to the grantor of the option that otherwise would constitute a counteroffer (and thereby a rejection of the original offer) without losing the right to exercise the option.
rules.short_rule_statement: The holder of an option can make an offer to the grantor of the option that otherwise would constitute a counteroffer (and thereby a rejection of the original offer) without...
rules.source_text:
Counteroffer Without Rejection
The holder of an option can make an offer to the grantor of the option that otherwise would constitute a counteroffer (and thereby a rejection of the original offer) without losing the right to exercise the option.
topic_path: Options And Rights Of First Refusal

components.content: The mailbox rule does not apply to the exercise of an option.  See the Themis Contracts outline
components.label: Exam Tip
components.source_text: Exam Tip: The mailbox rule does not apply to the exercise of an option.  See the Themis Contracts outline

rules.rule_statement: The holder of an option must exercise the option under the terms of the contract. The holder’s decision to exercise the option must be received by the grantor of the option within the period specified in the contract. § I.A.2.c.3 Mailbox Rule.
rules.short_rule_statement: The holder of an option must exercise the option under the terms of the contract. The holder’s decision to exercise the option must be received by the grantor of the option...
rules.source_text:
Acceptance
The holder of an option must exercise the option under the terms of the contract. The holder’s decision to exercise the option must be received by the grantor of the option within the period specified in the contract. § I.A.2.c.3 Mailbox Rule.
topic_path: Options And Rights Of First Refusal

rules.rule_statement: A right of first refusal is a preemptive right that gives its holder the opportunity to acquire property before its transfer to another, and it is valid unless it is unreasonable. The right may be limited in various ways, such as the period during which the right exists, the transactions to which it applies, and its transferability to another person.
rules.short_rule_statement: A right of first refusal is a preemptive right that gives its holder the opportunity to acquire property before its transfer to another, and it is valid unless it is...
rules.source_text:
Right Of First Refusal
A right of first refusal is a preemptive right that gives its holder the opportunity to acquire property before its transfer to another, and it is valid unless it is unreasonable. The right may be limited in various ways, such as the period during which the right exists, the transactions to which it applies, and its transferability to another person.
topic_path: Options And Rights Of First Refusal

rules.rule_statement: The Rule Against Perpetuities does not apply to an option or right of first refusal granted in a lease to a current leasehold tenant. At common law, the Rule would apply to options in gross, but the Third Restatement states that the Rule does not apply to options at all. In addition, under the Uniform Statutory Rule Against Perpetuities (USRAP), which has been adopted by a majority of jurisdictions, the Rule does not apply to an option contract or a right of first refusal when the property right is created in a commercial transaction. (See § I.B.10. Rule Against Perpetuities, supra.)
rules.short_rule_statement: The Rule Against Perpetuities does not apply to an option or right of first refusal granted in a lease to a current leasehold tenant. At common law, the Rule would apply to...
rules.source_text:
Rule Against Perpetuities
The Rule Against Perpetuities does not apply to an option or right of first refusal granted in a lease to a current leasehold tenant. At common law, the Rule would apply to options in gross, but the Third Restatement states that the Rule does not apply to options at all. In addition, under the Uniform Statutory Rule Against Perpetuities (USRAP), which has been adopted by a majority of jurisdictions, the Rule does not apply to an option contract or a right of first refusal when the property right is created in a commercial transaction. (See § I.B.10. Rule Against Perpetuities, supra.)
topic_path: Options And Rights Of First Refusal

rules.rule_statement: Although direct restraints on the alienability of property are invalid, both an option contract and a right of first refusal are only partial restraints. In determining whether to enforce each, the rule of reasonableness applies. Under this rule, the utility of the purpose served by the restraint is balanced against the likely harm that would result from its enforcement. In most cases, each is enforceable.
rules.short_rule_statement: Although direct restraints on the alienability of property are invalid, both an option contract and a right of first refusal are only partial restraints. In determining whether...
rules.source_text:
Restraints Against Alienability
Although direct restraints on the alienability of property are invalid, both an option contract and a right of first refusal are only partial restraints. In determining whether to enforce each, the rule of reasonableness applies. Under this rule, the utility of the purpose served by the restraint is balanced against the likely harm that would result from its enforcement. In most cases, each is enforceable.
topic_path: Options And Rights Of First Refusal

rules.rule_statement: A right of first refusal as well as an option contract can constitute an encumbrance that prevents the seller of property from providing marketable title. Restatement (Third) of Property: Servitudes § 3.4, cmt. c.
rules.short_rule_statement: A right of first refusal as well as an option contract can constitute an encumbrance that prevents the seller of property from providing marketable title. Restatement (Third)...
rules.source_text:
Marketable Title
A right of first refusal as well as an option contract can constitute an encumbrance that prevents the seller of property from providing marketable title. Restatement (Third) of Property: Servitudes § 3.4, cmt. c.
