rules.rule_statement: Under the doctrine of adverse possession, ownership of real property is transferred to a person who exercises exclusive physical possession of that property for a certain amount of time. Title acquired by adverse possession is as good as title transferred by a prior record owner. Government-owned land cannot be adversely possessed. Rule For possession to ripen into title, possession must be: Exclusive – physical presence on land not shared with the owner Continuous – presence is continuous and uninterrupted for statutory period Hostile – possession is without the owner’s consent Open, notorious, actual – possession is apparent or visible to a reasonable owner Remember the mnemonic ECHO (exclusive; continuous; hostile; open, notorious, and actual).
rules.short_rule_statement: Under the doctrine of adverse possession, ownership of real property is transferred to a person who exercises exclusive physical possession of that property for a certain...
rules.source_text:
Adverse Possession
Under the doctrine of adverse possession, ownership of real property is transferred to a person who exercises exclusive physical possession of that property for a certain amount of time. Title acquired by adverse possession is as good as title transferred by a prior record owner. Government-owned land cannot be adversely possessed. Rule For possession to ripen into title, possession must be: Exclusive – physical presence on land not shared with the owner Continuous – presence is continuous and uninterrupted for statutory period Hostile – possession is without the owner’s consent Open, notorious, actual – possession is apparent or visible to a reasonable owner Remember the mnemonic ECHO (exclusive; continuous; hostile; open, notorious, and actual).

rules.rule_statement: Possession cannot be shared with the true owner, although two or more people can join together to create a tenancy in common by adverse possession.
rules.short_rule_statement: Possession cannot be shared with the true owner, although two or more people can join together to create a tenancy in common by adverse possession.
rules.source_text:
Exclusive
Possession cannot be shared with the true owner, although two or more people can join together to create a tenancy in common by adverse possession.

rules.rule_statement: Possession must be continuous and uninterrupted for a specific period (e.g., 10 [many states], 15, or 20 years). Seasonal or infrequent use may be sufficiently continuous if it is consistent with the type of property that is being possessed (e.g., a vacation residence, a hunting camp).
rules.short_rule_statement: Possession must be continuous and uninterrupted for a specific period (e.g., 10 [many states], 15, or 20 years). Seasonal or infrequent use may be sufficiently continuous if it...
rules.source_text:
Continuous
Possession must be continuous and uninterrupted for a specific period (e.g., 10 [many states], 15, or 20 years). Seasonal or infrequent use may be sufficiently continuous if it is consistent with the type of property that is being possessed (e.g., a vacation residence, a hunting camp).

rules.rule_statement: An adverse possessor may tack on his predecessor’s time to satisfy the statutory period if there is privity between successive possessors. Privity is satisfied if the possessor takes by any nonhostile means (such as descent, devise, contract, or deed). The periods of possession must pass directly from one possessor to the next without any gaps. Tacking is not allowed when there is an actual, wrongful exclusion of a party entitled to possession from the property (ouster).
rules.short_rule_statement: An adverse possessor may tack on his predecessor’s time to satisfy the statutory period if there is privity between successive possessors. Privity is satisfied if the possessor...
rules.source_text:
Tacking
An adverse possessor may tack on his predecessor’s time to satisfy the statutory period if there is privity between successive possessors. Privity is satisfied if the possessor takes by any nonhostile means (such as descent, devise, contract, or deed). The periods of possession must pass directly from one possessor to the next without any gaps. Tacking is not allowed when there is an actual, wrongful exclusion of a party entitled to possession from the property (ouster).

rules.rule_statement: The statute of limitations will not run against a true owner who is afflicted with a disability (e.g., insanity, infancy, imprisonment) at the inception of the adverse possession.
rules.short_rule_statement: The statute of limitations will not run against a true owner who is afflicted with a disability (e.g., insanity, infancy, imprisonment) at the inception of the adverse possession.
rules.source_text:
Disability Of Owner
The statute of limitations will not run against a true owner who is afflicted with a disability (e.g., insanity, infancy, imprisonment) at the inception of the adverse possession.

rules.rule_statement: Once adverse possession has begun, a transfer of the property to another owner (e.g., sale, gift, devise) will not restart the adverse possession period.
rules.short_rule_statement: Once adverse possession has begun, a transfer of the property to another owner (e.g., sale, gift, devise) will not restart the adverse possession period.
rules.source_text:
Transfer To Another Owner
Once adverse possession has begun, a transfer of the property to another owner (e.g., sale, gift, devise) will not restart the adverse possession period.

rules.rule_statement: The adverse possessor must possess the land without the owner’s permission. The majority of jurisdictions do not require that the possession be hostile in the sense that the possessor purposefully seeks to defeat the owner’s title. Instead, the possessor, by possessing the land, must objectively demonstrate an intent to claim the land as his own, regardless of his subjective intent. In jurisdictions that consider the possessor’s subjective intent, some require a possessor to believe, in good faith, that the possessor has the legal right to possess the property (i.e., believes that the property is not owned or thinks that the possessor owns the property). Other states require the possessor to act in bad faith (i.e., aggressive trespass).
rules.short_rule_statement: The adverse possessor must possess the land without the owner’s permission. The majority of jurisdictions do not require that the possession be hostile in the sense that the...
rules.source_text:
Hostile
The adverse possessor must possess the land without the owner’s permission. The majority of jurisdictions do not require that the possession be hostile in the sense that the possessor purposefully seeks to defeat the owner’s title. Instead, the possessor, by possessing the land, must objectively demonstrate an intent to claim the land as his own, regardless of his subjective intent. In jurisdictions that consider the possessor’s subjective intent, some require a possessor to believe, in good faith, that the possessor has the legal right to possess the property (i.e., believes that the property is not owned or thinks that the possessor owns the property). Other states require the possessor to act in bad faith (i.e., aggressive trespass).

rules.rule_statement: Actual possession requires physical presence by the adverse possessor or another whose possession is attributable to the adverse possessor. A person’s possession may be attributable to the adverse possessor when, for example, the adverse possessor leases his interest to that person (i.e., a landlord-tenant relationship exists). Possession must be open and notorious such that a reasonable true owner would become aware of the claim. Uses that are hidden are insufficient to satisfy this requirement.
rules.short_rule_statement: Actual possession requires physical presence by the adverse possessor or another whose possession is attributable to the adverse possessor. A person’s possession may be...
rules.source_text:
Open, Notorious, And Actual
Actual possession requires physical presence by the adverse possessor or another whose possession is attributable to the adverse possessor. A person’s possession may be attributable to the adverse possessor when, for example, the adverse possessor leases his interest to that person (i.e., a landlord-tenant relationship exists). Possession must be open and notorious such that a reasonable true owner would become aware of the claim. Uses that are hidden are insufficient to satisfy this requirement.

rules.rule_statement: If a person enters property under color of title (i.e., a facially valid will or deed) and actually possesses only a portion of the property, then constructive adverse possession can give title to the whole. The amount possessed must be a reasonable portion of the whole.
rules.short_rule_statement: If a person enters property under color of title (i.e., a facially valid will or deed) and actually possesses only a portion of the property, then constructive adverse...
rules.source_text:
Constructive Adverse Possession
If a person enters property under color of title (i.e., a facially valid will or deed) and actually possesses only a portion of the property, then constructive adverse possession can give title to the whole. The amount possessed must be a reasonable portion of the whole.

rules.rule_statement: The adverse possessor of the surface acquires the rights to the subsurface (e.g., mineral rights), unless those rights belong to a third party.
rules.short_rule_statement: The adverse possessor of the surface acquires the rights to the subsurface (e.g., mineral rights), unless those rights belong to a third party.
rules.source_text:
Below The Surface Area
The adverse possessor of the surface acquires the rights to the subsurface (e.g., mineral rights), unless those rights belong to a third party.

rules.rule_statement: The adverse possessor acquires the estate held by the person who has legal possession when the adverse possession began. The adverse possession period does not run against a future interest that exists when the adverse possession begins, but it does begin to run against a future interest created from a fee simple absolute estate after the adverse possession has begun.
rules.short_rule_statement: The adverse possessor acquires the estate held by the person who has legal possession when the adverse possession began. The adverse possession period does not run against a...
rules.source_text:
Future Interests
The adverse possessor acquires the estate held by the person who has legal possession when the adverse possession began. The adverse possession period does not run against a future interest that exists when the adverse possession begins, but it does begin to run against a future interest created from a fee simple absolute estate after the adverse possession has begun.

rules.canonical_name: Delivery And Recording Of A Deed
rules.rule_statement: To transfer a real property interest, the grantor must demonstrate the intent to make a present transfer of the interest, and the grantee must accept the interest. For purposes of this rule, delivery of the deed is a method for demonstrating the intent to make a present transfer of the interest. A deed is the legal instrument that transfers the ownership of real property. In addition, pursuant to the Statute of Frauds, the transfer of a real property interest must be evidenced by a writing. In other words, there must be a written contract or deed satisfying the Statue of Frauds and establishing the essential terms. Finally, the grantee may be able to protect his property interest against others’ claims by recording the deed.
rules.short_rule_statement: To transfer a real property interest, the grantor must demonstrate the intent to make a present transfer of the interest, and the grantee must accept the interest. For purposes...
rules.source_text:
Delivery And Recording Of A Deed
To transfer a real property interest, the grantor must demonstrate the intent to make a present transfer of the interest, and the grantee must accept the interest. For purposes of this rule, delivery of the deed is a method for demonstrating the intent to make a present transfer of the interest. A deed is the legal instrument that transfers the ownership of real property. In addition, pursuant to the Statute of Frauds, the transfer of a real property interest must be evidenced by a writing. In other words, there must be a written contract or deed satisfying the Statue of Frauds and establishing the essential terms. Finally, the grantee may be able to protect his property interest against others’ claims by recording the deed.
topic_path: Delivery And Recording Of A Deed

rules.rule_statement: The grantor must, at the time of transfer, intend to make a present transfer of a property interest to the grantee. Note that the interest itself may be a future interest, such as when a parent retains a life estate in a residence and transfers a remainder interest to an adult child. Typically, this intent is manifested by delivery of the deed. Delivery may be completed by physically handing or mailing the deed to the grantee or the grantee’s agent. Although it is often stated that a deed must be delivered for a real property interest to pass (i.e., a delivery requirement), the term “delivery” is used as shorthand for the existence of the necessary grantor intent. Physical transfer of a deed is not required and is not conclusive evidence of the grantor’s intent.
rules.short_rule_statement: The grantor must, at the time of transfer, intend to make a present transfer of a property interest to the grantee. Note that the interest itself may be a future interest, such...
rules.source_text:
Intent To Transfer (Delivery)
The grantor must, at the time of transfer, intend to make a present transfer of a property interest to the grantee. Note that the interest itself may be a future interest, such as when a parent retains a life estate in a residence and transfers a remainder interest to an adult child. Typically, this intent is manifested by delivery of the deed. Delivery may be completed by physically handing or mailing the deed to the grantee or the grantee’s agent. Although it is often stated that a deed must be delivered for a real property interest to pass (i.e., a delivery requirement), the term “delivery” is used as shorthand for the existence of the necessary grantor intent. Physical transfer of a deed is not required and is not conclusive evidence of the grantor’s intent.
topic_path: Delivery And Recording Of A Deed

rules.canonical_name: Retention Of The Deed By Grantor
rules.rule_statement: When the grantor keeps the deed, there is a presumption that the grantor lacked the intent to transfer the property, but parol evidence is admissible to establish that the grantor had the intent to make a present transfer of the property interest. For example, the recording of an executed deed by the grantor or his agent rebuts the initial presumption and creates a rebuttable presumption that the grantor had the necessary intent to transfer ownership of the property.
rules.short_rule_statement: When the grantor keeps the deed, there is a presumption that the grantor lacked the intent to transfer the property, but parol evidence is admissible to establish that the...
rules.source_text:
Retention Of The Deed By Grantor
When the grantor keeps the deed, there is a presumption that the grantor lacked the intent to transfer the property, but parol evidence is admissible to establish that the grantor had the intent to make a present transfer of the property interest. For example, the recording of an executed deed by the grantor or his agent rebuts the initial presumption and creates a rebuttable presumption that the grantor had the necessary intent to transfer ownership of the property.
topic_path: Delivery And Recording Of A Deed
Retention Of The Deed By Grantor

rules.rule_statement: Transfer of a deed to the grantee creates a presumption that the grantor intended to make a present transfer of the property interest. Parol evidence is admissible to show that the grantor lacks such intent (e.g., the grantor intended only to create a mortgage, not to effect an outright transfer). However, when the grantor transferred the deed to the grantee subject to an oral condition (i.e., a condition that does not appear in the deed), parol evidence is not admissible, and the condition is not enforceable. Although physical transfer of a deed is not conclusive evidence of the grantor’s intent to transfer a real property interest, a grantor without the intent to transfer real property who nonetheless allows the grantee to obtain physical possession of the deed may be estopped from asserting that the delivery was invalid against a bona fide purchaser from the grantee.
rules.short_rule_statement: Transfer of a deed to the grantee creates a presumption that the grantor intended to make a present transfer of the property interest. Parol evidence is admissible to show that...
rules.source_text:
Transfer Of Deed To Grantee
Transfer of a deed to the grantee creates a presumption that the grantor intended to make a present transfer of the property interest. Parol evidence is admissible to show that the grantor lacks such intent (e.g., the grantor intended only to create a mortgage, not to effect an outright transfer). However, when the grantor transferred the deed to the grantee subject to an oral condition (i.e., a condition that does not appear in the deed), parol evidence is not admissible, and the condition is not enforceable. Although physical transfer of a deed is not conclusive evidence of the grantor’s intent to transfer a real property interest, a grantor without the intent to transfer real property who nonetheless allows the grantee to obtain physical possession of the deed may be estopped from asserting that the delivery was invalid against a bona fide purchaser from the grantee.

rules.canonical_name: Transfer Of Deed To Third Party
rules.rule_statement: When the grantor transfers the deed to her own agent (e.g., an attorney), the transfer is treated as if the grantor had retained the deed, even when the grantor has instructed the agent to deliver the deed to the grantee at some future time or upon the happening of an event. Until the grantor’s agent delivers the deed to the grantee, the grantor can demand that the agent ignore the prior instruction and return the deed to the grantor. When the grantor transfers the deed to the grantee’s agent, the transfer is treated as if it had been made to the grantee herself. If the grantor purportedly gives property to a grantee through a third party and places a condition on the transfer of the deed by the third party to the grantee, whether delivery has occurred depends on the grantor’s language. If the grantor retains an absolute right to recover the deed, then no valid delivery exists because transfer of title was not clearly intended. Example 1: Party A executes a deed that transfers real property to B. The deed itself contains no conditions. Party A gives the deed to C to hold and says, “Give this deed to B on his 21st birthday, unless I ask for the deed back before then.” Because A retained an absolute right to recover the deed, A did not have the intent to make a present transfer. If the grantor does not retain a right to retrieve the deed, then the key is whether the grantor intends to make a present gift of a property interest. If so, the grantor cannot later void the gift. Instead, the conditional transfer is treated as creating a future property interest in the grantee. Example 2: Party A executes a deed that transfers real property to B. The deed itself contains no conditions. Party A gives the deed to an independent third party, C, and says, “I want B to have this property when she has her first child. Please give the deed to her then.” Because A gave C the deed with the intent to presently create an executory interest in B, B currently has an executory interest in the real property. When the third party’s transfer of the deed to the grantee is conditioned on the grantor’s death, the grantor’s transfer of the deed to the third party must evidence the intent to make a present gift. Example: Party A executes a deed that transfers real property to B. The deed itself contains no conditions. Party A gives the deed to an independent third party, C, saying, “I want B to have this property upon my death. Please give the deed to her then.” Because A gave C the deed with the intent to presently create a remainder interest in B, the transfer is effective. If the grantor’s intent is that the gift itself be effective only upon the grantor’s death, the grantor may create a death escrow. A death escrow is a method of transferring property outside of probate by delivering the deed to an escrow agent to be held until the grantor’s death. To be effective, the transfer cannot be revocable by the grantor, and it must comply with the requirements for a testamentary transfer (i.e., the Statute of Wills). Example: Party A executes a deed that transfers real property to B. The deed itself contains no conditions. Party A gives the deed to an independent third party, C, saying, “I want B to have this property if she outlives me. Please give the deed to her then.” Because A gave C the deed with the intent to make a testamentary transfer (i.e., requiring that B survive A to take the property), the transfer is ineffective unless the requirements for a testamentary transfer were satisfied. When a contract for the sale of real property calls for the seller to give the deed to an independent third party (i.e., an escrow agent) and conditions the release of the deed to the buyer on the happening of an event, typically payment of the purchase price, the escrow agent is obligated to transfer the deed to the buyer if the condition occurs.

rules.rule_statement: When there is a written contract, the grantor cannot require the escrow agent to return the deed before the failure of the condition to occur. When the contract is oral, the grantor can reclaim the deed from the escrow agent because the Statute of Frauds requires a writing for a land-sales contract to be enforceable.

Retrieval By Grantor
When there is a written contract, the grantor cannot require the escrow agent to return the deed before the failure of the condition to occur. When the contract is oral, the grantor can reclaim the deed from the escrow agent because the Statute of Frauds requires a writing for a land-sales contract to be enforceable.

rules.rule_statement: When the escrow agent delivers the deed to the buyer before the performance of the condition, title to the property remains with the seller. This is true even when the buyer then transfers the property to a third party, even a person who purchases the property in good faith (i.e., a bona fide purchaser). An exception exists when the seller permits the buyer to possess the property.

rules.source_text:
Escrow Agent
When the escrow agent delivers the deed to the buyer before the performance of the condition, title to the property remains with the seller. This is true even when the buyer then transfers the property to a third party, even a person who purchases the property in good faith (i.e., a bona fide purchaser). An exception exists when the seller permits the buyer to possess the property.

rules.rule_statement: In general, title to the property remains in the seller until the condition is satisfied. Once the condition is satisfied, title automatically vests in the buyer. The date of transfer can relate back to the date that the grantor deposited the deed in escrow when, before the condition is satisfied, the grantor dies, the grantor becomes incapacitated or marries, or the grantee dies.

rules.source_text:
Time Of Transfer
In general, title to the property remains in the seller until the condition is satisfied. Once the condition is satisfied, title automatically vests in the buyer. The date of transfer can relate back to the date that the grantor deposited the deed in escrow when, before the condition is satisfied, the grantor dies, the grantor becomes incapacitated or marries, or the grantee dies.

rules.rule_statement: Acceptance is required for a transfer to be complete, and the grantee is generally presumed to have accepted any beneficial conveyance. Acceptance relates back to the time the deed was transferred unless a bona fide purchaser or creditor of the grantor would be negatively affected by doing so. If the grantee rejects the deed, no title passes, and the grantor holds the title. If the grantee accepts the deed and then changes his mind even a moment later, his return, destruction, or cancellation of the deed is ineffective to transfer ownership of the property back to the grantor. A new, valid deed must be executed for the grantee to convey the property back to the original grantor.

rules.rule_statement: An instrument that passes an interest in real property (e.g., fee simple, easement) is a deed.

rules.source_text:
Deed
An instrument that passes an interest in real property (e.g., fee simple, easement) is a deed.

rules.canonical_name: Drafting, Review, And Negotiation Of Deed And Other Closing Documents
rules.rule_statement: In general, the giving of legal advice and counsel to others, as well as the preparation and approval of legal instruments such as deeds and mortgages for someone else, constitutes the practice of law. However, a nonattorney who is acting on his own behalf may select and draft a legal document. (See § III.A.1.c. Preparation of Contract of Sale, supra, regarding the completion of a standard-form contract by a real estate agent or broker.) The completion of standard mortgage loan documents by nonattorney employees of the mortgage lender is not the practice of law, even if the lender charges the borrower a reasonable fee for preparing such documents, so long as the employees are not exercising legal discretion in completing the documents. A slight majority of states do not require an attorney to conduct a settlement (i.e., closing). However, although a nonattorney may conduct a settlement, the nonattorney may not answer legal questions that arise or otherwise give legal advice.

rules.rule_statement: A valid deed must include all necessary terms: the identity of the grantor and the grantee, words of transfer, a description of the property interest being transferred, and the grantor’s signature. Unlike in a contract, consideration is not required. The grantor and grantee must be identified. A deed that does not identify the grantee is ineffective until the grantee’s name is added or determined. A deed to a nonexistent grantee is void as to the nonexistent grantee. Example: A grantor executed a deed conveying Blackacre “to my neighbors.” Until the intended neighbors are determined by extrinsic evidence, the deed has no effect. The portion of a deed that contains the words that transfer an interest from the grantor to the grantee is called a “granting clause” (e.g., “do grant and convey”). Any language evidencing a present intent to transfer will suffice. The description of the property must be reasonably definite, but extrinsic evidence is admissible to clarify. A habendum clause (e.g., “to have and to hold”) in a deed defines the interest conveyed. When there is an irreconcilable difference between the granting clause and the habendum clause, the granting clause prevails. Descriptions by metes and bounds or by street address are acceptable but not required to sufficiently describe the property. When there is a conflict between descriptions of the property in the deed, descriptions based on monuments (e.g., “from oak tree east to the tool shed”) are given priority over area descriptions (e.g., “100 acres”).

rules.rule_statement: A grantor’s signature on the deed is generally required, but in most states, it need not be witnessed or acknowledged (e.g., notarized). Although a grantee’s signature is not required (a deed that is not signed by the grantee is sometimes referred to as a “deed poll”), the grantee’s acceptance of the deed is sufficient to make easements and covenants in the deed enforceable against the grantee.

Deed Execution
A grantor’s signature on the deed is generally required, but in most states, it need not be witnessed or acknowledged (e.g., notarized). Although a grantee’s signature is not required (a deed that is not signed by the grantee is sometimes referred to as a “deed poll”), the grantee’s acceptance of the deed is sufficient to make easements and covenants in the deed enforceable against the grantee.

rules.rule_statement: A deed that contains a grantor’s forged signature is void. Similarly, a deed that has been forged is void. Neither is effective, even if relied upon by a bona fide purchaser.
rules.short_rule_statement: A deed that contains a grantor’s forged signature is void. Similarly, a deed that has been forged is void. Neither is effective, even if relied upon by a bona fide purchaser.
rules.source_text:
Forgery
A deed that contains a grantor’s forged signature is void. Similarly, a deed that has been forged is void. Neither is effective, even if relied upon by a bona fide purchaser.

rules.rule_statement: As with a contract, a deed procured through fraud in the factum (i.e., fraud in the execution), which occurs when the fraudulent misrepresentation prevents a party from knowing that the document is a deed, is void. Such a deed is not effective, even if relied upon by a bona fide purchaser. By contrast, a deed procured through fraud in the inducement, which occurs when a fraudulent misrepresentation is used to induce the grantor to execute the deed, is voidable by the grantor if she justifiably relied on the misrepresentation in executing the deed. However, a bona fide purchaser without notice of the fraud may enforce the deed. An agent may execute a deed on behalf of a principal-grantor.

rules.source_text:
Fraud
As with a contract, a deed procured through fraud in the factum (i.e., fraud in the execution), which occurs when the fraudulent misrepresentation prevents a party from knowing that the document is a deed, is void. Such a deed is not effective, even if relied upon by a bona fide purchaser. By contrast, a deed procured through fraud in the inducement, which occurs when a fraudulent misrepresentation is used to induce the grantor to execute the deed, is voidable by the grantor if she justifiably relied on the misrepresentation in executing the deed. However, a bona fide purchaser without notice of the fraud may enforce the deed. An agent may execute a deed on behalf of a principal-grantor.

rules.rule_statement: While the creation of an agency relationship does not generally require a writing, under the equal-dignities rule, when the act performed by an agent on behalf of the principal is required to be in writing by law, the agent’s authority must also be in writing. Consequently, when an agent signs a deed (or a real property instrument that is subject to the Statute of Frauds, such as a contract for the sale of real property), the agent’s authority must also be in writing. An officer of an entity such as a corporation who has the authority to act on behalf of the entity by virtue of the officer’s status may sign a deed without the corporation’s specific authorization to do so. In addition, a person whose only act is signing a specific deed at the grantor’s request (i.e., an amanuensis) may do so without written authorization. A principal-grantor may be estopped from asserting the lack of writing as a defense when the principal has made a manifestation of an agent’s authority that induces detrimental reliance by a third party.

components.content: Grantor’s lack of capacity and other defenses
components.source_text: 3)      Grantor’s lack of capacity and other defenses

rules.rule_statement: A deed is valid if an agent with authority signs only the principal’s name. A deed is not valid at law if an agent with authority signs only her own name, but the deed may be enforced in equity as between the parties and subsequent purchasers with notice of the existence of the agency. A grantor who conveys a real property interest is subject to the same capacity requirements as a person who enters into a contract. In addition, a deed may be subject to reformation or rescission on the same grounds as a contract (e.g., fraud, undue influence, duress). Crossover Topic: Contracts See the Themis Contracts outline § I.E.6 Capacity to Contract and § VIII.G.4 Grounds for Reformation and Recission. Generally, a cotenant in a joint tenancy or a tenancy in common can convey only his interest in jointly owned property. A deed executed by a cotenant that purports to be a conveyance of the entire jointly owned property has the effect of conveying only that cotenant’s interest. A purported conveyance of a physical portion of jointly owned property is void.

rules.rule_statement: While a deed need not be recorded to be valid and convey good title to the transferee, all states have enacted recording acts, which establish priorities among conflicting claims to real property interests and promote certainty of title. Nearly all instruments affecting real property interests may be recorded, including easements, covenants, leases, contracts to convey, and mortgages. The best practice is to properly record real property interests. Recording acts reward interest holders who record their real property interests because recording an interest gives notice to future potential buyers. This promotes transparency in real estate transactions and enables conveyances that are free from unexpected encumbrances. For purposes of the recording act, most jurisdictions also require the grantor to acknowledge the deed or other instrument (e.g., a notarized deed) to prevent fraudulent transfers. But most jurisdictions have a adopted a curative statute that requires a challenge to a missing or defective acknowledgment to be timely brought (e.g., within two years of recording). Unless the recording act governs, the common-law rule of “first in time, first in right” generally applies to determine priorities. A failure to record does not affect the validity of the instrument between the parties to the instrument. For example, a buyer’s failure to record the deed does not preclude the buyer from suing his seller for unjust enrichment when the seller resells the same property to a third party.

Recording Act
While a deed need not be recorded to be valid and convey good title to the transferee, all states have enacted recording acts, which establish priorities among conflicting claims to real property interests and promote certainty of title. Nearly all instruments affecting real property interests may be recorded, including easements, covenants, leases, contracts to convey, and mortgages. The best practice is to properly record real property interests. Recording acts reward interest holders who record their real property interests because recording an interest gives notice to future potential buyers. This promotes transparency in real estate transactions and enables conveyances that are free from unexpected encumbrances. For purposes of the recording act, most jurisdictions also require the grantor to acknowledge the deed or other instrument (e.g., a notarized deed) to prevent fraudulent transfers. But most jurisdictions have a adopted a curative statute that requires a chall

rules.rule_statement: The three types of recording statutes are notice, race, and race-notice. EXAM NOTE: Notice and race-notice statutes are the most frequently tested types of recording statutes on the bar exam. A purchaser need only purchase without notice of the prior interest to prevail under a notice statute. (Notice statutes tend to protect subsequent purchasers against interest holders who could have but failed to record documents describing their interests.) EXAM NOTE: Notice statutes are commonly tested and usually contain words such as “in good faith” or “without notice.” Example of a notice statute: “No conveyance or mortgage of real property shall be good against subsequent purchasers for value and without notice unless the same be recorded according to law.” EXAM NOTE: Remember that a purchaser need not record to prevail over a prior interest in a notice jurisdiction, but the purchaser must record to prevail against a subsequent purchaser. A minority of states have race statutes, under which a purchaser who records first prevails, regardless of his knowledge of any prior conflicting interests. Example of a race statute: “No conveyance or mortgage of real property shall be good against subsequent purchasers for value unless the same be first recorded according to law.” A race-notice statute requires a subsequent purchaser to take the interest without notice of a prior conflicting interest and be the first to record. EXAM NOTE: Look for words in the statute such as “in good faith” in conjunction with phrases such as “ﬁrst duly recorded.” Example of a race-notice statute: “No conveyance or mortgage of real property shall be good against subsequent purchasers for value and without notice who shall first record.” EXAM NOTE: The recording acts provide that prior conveyances and mortgages that would otherwise prevail under the common-law “ﬁrst in time, ﬁrst in right” rule may lose priority to certain “subsequent purchasers for value” who meet the requirements of the recording act (i.e., took without notice, recorded ﬁrst, or both). In other words, the recording acts protect the claim of certain later purchasers who would have lost under the common-law rule.  But remember that when a later purchaser does not meet the requirements of the recording act, the common-law “ﬁrst in time, ﬁrst in right” rule applies.

A minority of states have race statu

rules.rule_statement: Only a grantee who pays value for an interest in real property is entitled to protection under the recording statutes. A grantee need not pay fair market value of the real property interest, but most jurisdictions require the payment of a substantial amount that is not grossly inadequate relative to the value of the property interest. A mere nominal amount that is sufficient to serve as consideration for the formation of a contract is not sufficient to trigger the protection of a recording act. In addition, a promise to pay is not sufficient unless the promise to pay is in the form of a negotiable instrument. Mortgagees are considered to have paid value and are protected by the recording acts if the mortgage is given simultaneously with a loan or there is a change in the terms of a prior loan favorable to the borrower (e.g., lower interest rate, extended repayment period). Grantees who acquire title of property by gift, intestacy, or devise are not protected by the recording act against prior claims to the same property, even when those claims are not recorded. Example: Party A sells Blackacre to B, who forgets to record the deed. In the interim, A gives a deed to his daughter, C, who promptly records her deed. Party C will not defeat B’s ownership because C did not pay value for the property. Creditors are protected only against claims that arise after a judgment against the debtor is recorded, unless otherwise indicated by statute. Note that a lawsuit may be pending that could affect title to the property. In such case, any party to the action may record a lis pendens (a notice of pending action), which serves as notice to third parties of the claims pending in the lawsuit. Example: Party B makes a loan to A, and A grants B a mortgage interest in Blackacre as security for the loan. Party C, a creditor of A, not knowing of the deed to B, then records a judgment lien against Blackacre. Party C is not protected against B’s unrecorded interest because it preceded the recording of C’s lien. Most jurisdictions protect a purchaser of property at a judicial sale against all unrecorded interests subject to the recording act. Example: Assume the same facts as those in the example above. Party C obtains an order requiring a sheriff to levy on the lien, and Blackacre is sold at a judicial sale. Party D purchases Blackacre at the sale. Party D takes Blackacre free of B’s mortgage interest. Grantors who are protected by the recording act protect (or “shelter”) their grantees who would otherwise be unprotected. The exception to the shelter rule is that a purchaser who has notice of a prior property interest cannot sell the property interest to a person without such notice and then buy back the property to gain the protection of the shelter rule.

rules.rule_statement: Only purchasers who give value in good faith and without notice of a prior claim will prevail in notice or race-notice jurisdictions. Notice can be actual, inquiry, or record notice. Whether a purchaser has notice is tested as of the time of the conveyance of the property to the purchaser. Notice obtained by the purchaser after the conveyance does not prevent the purchaser from enjoying the benefit of the recording act. A grantee possessing actual, personal knowledge of a prior interest cannot prevail under a notice or race-notice recording statute. If a reasonable investigation would have disclosed the existence of prior claims, then the grantee is considered to possess inquiry notice, and she cannot prevail against those prior claims. The purchaser is charged with whatever knowledge a reasonable inspection of the property would have disclosed. In most states, taking a quitclaim deed does not in itself create inquiry notice of prior claims. EXAM NOTE: Examples of situations that typically give rise to inquiry notice are (1) someone other than the grantor has possession of the property (e.g., a tenant) or (2) documents are referenced in the chain of title. Example: Party A’s deed to B references a restrictive covenant entered into by A and B at the time of the deed. The deed is recorded, but the covenant is not contained in the deed. Any purchaser in A’s chain of title will be charged with knowledge of the covenant and its contents. Grantees are held to have constructive notice of all prior conveyances that were properly recorded.

rules.rule_statement: In a jurisdiction that maintains grantor, grantee indexes, the real property documents are organized chronologically based on the date that the document is received by the recording entity (e.g., county land records office). To search for the actual documents, two indexes are maintained; one lists the last name of the grantors, and another lists the names of the grantees. Each index identifies the actual document associated with the grantor or grantee. When searching the chain of title to ensure good title, the potential purchaser must first search for the seller’s name as a grantee in the grantee index to discover when the seller acquired the property interest. The purchaser must then search for the name of the seller’s grantor as a grantee, and so on, until the title has been searched back to its inception (common-law rule) or as far back as the law requires. Then, the earliest grantee’s name is searched under the grantor index to discover if and when the grantee conveyed the property interest to someone else. Next, that grantee’s name is searched under the grantor’s index to determine if and when that grantee conveyed the property interest to someone else, and so on. About half of the states have adopted a marketable title act. The act establishes a search cutoff date that limits the period within which a property interest must be searched (e.g., 40 years). A property interest that is not reflected in a recorded document (e.g., deed or mortgage) within that period lapses unless it is rerecorded. Under the majority rule (i.e., legal blinders rule), a title searcher is required to search only as far back as necessary to validate the searcher’s title. A person must search the real property records for records affecting that person’s real property interest that would be within that person’s chain of title but may wear “legal blinders” regarding records that fall outside the chain of title. Under the minority rule, a title searcher must search the entire real property records regarding a person who identified as having a property interest, subject to any time restriction imposed by a marketable title act; a recorded document that falls outside the chain of title nevertheless gives record notice of the property interest. When the registrar or court clerk fails to index an instrument, the traditional rule is that the recording of the instrument is sufficient to give constructive notice of the unindexed instrument, while the modern view is that there is no constructive notice of this instrument. When the registrar or court clerk incorrectly indexes an instrument or the instrument itself fails to correctly identify a party, most jurisdictions, adopting the doctrine of idem sonans (i.e., treatment of similarly sounding words [e.g., “Reed” and “Reid”] as the same for legal purposes), require a search of similar sounding names. However, the modern trend is to permit a title searcher to rely on the names as spelled in the documents. Some jurisdictions do not treat a Mother Hubbard clause (i.e., an omnibus clause in an instrument that fails to identify specific property or that, in addition to identifying specific property, includes other nonspecific property) as giving constructive notice as to the property not specifically identified.

rules.rule_statement: Example 1: Party O sells Blackacre to A, but A does not record. Then, O sells Blackacre to B, who has no notice of the earlier conveyance to A. Next, A records. Then, B records. Party B sues A to quiet title in Blackacre. What is the result? ii) Under a notice statute, regardless of who records first, those who in good faith purchase without notice prevail. Party B wins because B took without notice. iii) Under a race-notice statute, Party B is unable to prove that B had no notice and recorded first. Because A recorded first, A wins. Example 2: Party O sells an easement in Blackacre to A, but A does not record. Then, O sells Blackacre to B, who knows of the earlier conveyance to A. Next, B records. Then, A records. Party B sues A to quiet title to Blackacre. What is the result? ii) Under a notice statute, A wins because B had notice of A’s rights at the time of purchase. iii) Under a race-notice statute, A prevails and can enforce the easement against B because B is unable to show that B both recorded first and purchased without notice of A’s prior claim. Example 3: Party O grants a mortgage on Blackacre to A in exchange for a loan, but A does not record her mortgage. Then, O grants a mortgage on Blackacre to B in exchange for a loan. Party B does not have notice of A’s mortgage. Then, A records her mortgage before B records his mortgage. For value, B assigns his mortgage to C, who is aware of A’s mortgage. Party C records the assignment of B’s mortgage to her. Whose mortgage has priority? ii) Under a notice statute, C’s mortgage has priority, even though C had notice of A’s mortgage because of the shelter rule. Party B took his mortgage without notice of A’s mortgage. Therefore, B’s mortgage had priority over A’s mortgage, and C, as assignee of B’s mortgage, is entitled to that priority as well. iii) Under a race-notice statute, A’s mortgage has priority because A recorded her mortgage first.

rules.rule_statement: Although an instrument is recorded and indexed in the recording office, it may not be recorded in such a way as to give notice to subsequent purchasers (i.e., the deed may not be in the chain of title). A recorded deed that is not within the chain of title is a wild deed. Example: Party O sells Blackacre to A, but A does not record. Then, A sells Blackacre to B, and B records. Then, O conveys Blackacre to C, who has no notice of the earlier conveyances to A or B. Next, C records. Then, A records the deed from O to A. Party B sues C for title to Blackacre. What is the result? C because the deed from A to B was a wild deed, outside C’s chain of title. In performing a title search, C would have searched in the grantor index for deeds listing O as the grantor from the date the deed granted Blackacre to O to the date C recorded the deed from O to C. No deed from O to A would have been discovered in such a search, and without finding a deed from O to A, C would not be expected to look for a deed from A to B. ii) Under a notice statute, C prevails because C had no actual notice of the conveyances from O to A and from A to B and the deed from A to B did not give him constructive notice because it was a wild deed. iii) Under a race-notice statute, C prevails for a combination of the two reasons above; C had no actual or constructive notice of O’s deed to A, and B’s prior recording of the deed from A to B does not count because it is a wild deed. Example: Party O sells Blackacre to A, but A does not record. Then, O sells Blackacre to B, who has actual notice of the conveyance from O to A, and B records. Then, A records. Next, B conveys to C, who has no actual notice of the O-to-A conveyance. Then, C records. Party C sues A for title to Blackacre. What is the result? of title. Remember that under the majority rule, C would research the grantor index under O’s name only until the date that B recorded the deed from O to B. Because O’s deed to A was recorded after that date, it is not considered duly recorded for the purposes of a race statute. ii) Under a notice statute, C wins because C had no notice and because A’s deed was recorded outside C’s chain of title. iii) Under a race-notice statute, C wins because both race and notice requirements are met. Example: The record reveals that O owns Blackacre. Party A sells Blackacre to B. Party B records the general warranty deed. Party O transfers Blackacre to A. Party A records. Party A sells Blackacre to C. Party C does not have actual or inquiry notice of A-to-B deed. Party C records. Party B sues C for title to Blackacre. What is the result? because A did not have title to Blackacre, when A did gain title to Blackacre through the O-to-A deed, A’s title became B’s title by virtue of the estoppel-by-deed doctrine and after-acquired property rule. (See § 5.a.3).d) After- Acquired Title, below.) Consequently, B’s recorded title became effective then, which predates C recording his title. (Note: Party B’s record knowledge of O’s deed does not matter under a race statute.) ii) Under a notice statute, C wins because the A-to-B deed was recorded outside C’s chain of title. Remember that under the majority rule, C would begin researching the grantor index under A’s name only from the date that A gained title to Blackacre. Because the A-to-B deed was recorded before that date, it is not considered duly recorded for the purposes of a notice statute and did not therefore put C on notice of B’s interest in Blackacre. iii) In a race-notice jurisdiction, B wins because although C does not have record notice of the A-to-B deed, B recorded first.

rules.rule_statement: A recording act does not protect a subsequent purchaser for value from a property interest acquired by operation of law, such as a property interest acquired by adverse possession or prescriptive easement. Because generally such ownership arises automatically when the statutory period expires, there is no documentation to be recorded. However, for an unrecorded easement, including an easement created by prescription, implication, estoppel, or oral grant, the Restatement (Third) has taken the position that these easements are subject to extinguishment under a recording act unless the easement is reasonably necessary for the enjoyment of the dominant estate, relates to an underground utility, or, at least when the recording act is a race-notice or notice type act, the easement would be discovered by reasonable inspection or inquiry. When a deed would not be given effect because of the grantee’s actions, such as a forged deed or a deed with a forged grantor’s signature, the recording act does not validate such a deed. Example: An owner of land promises to transfer it to her nephew. The owner prepares a deed but does not deliver it to the nephew. After the owner’s death, the nephew finds the deed among the owner’s papers and records it. The deed is ineffective to transfer ownership to the nephew because it was not delivered to the nephew during the owner’s lifetime. The nephew’s recording of the deed does not validate this deed.

rules.rule_statement: After conducting a title search, a title insurance company typically issues a title commitment that binds the company to provide title insurance to the purchaser of real property or a mortgagee. The commitment may list matters, such as an outstanding mortgage, that must be corrected before the company will issue a policy. After closing, the deed and any new mortgages are recorded, and the title insurance policy is issued.
rules.short_rule_statement: After conducting a title search, a title insurance company typically issues a title commitment that binds the company to provide title insurance to the purchaser of real...
rules.source_text:
Process
After conducting a title search, a title insurance company typically issues a title commitment that binds the company to provide title insurance to the purchaser of real property or a mortgagee. The commitment may list matters, such as an outstanding mortgage, that must be corrected before the company will issue a policy. After closing, the deed and any new mortgages are recorded, and the title insurance policy is issued.

rules.rule_statement: Title insurance typically protects against not only defects that could have been uncovered by a careful title search, such as recorded easements, covenants, mortgages, and tax or judgment liens, but also title defects resulting from forged instruments, undelivered deeds, or deeds executed by a person without legal capacity or authority. Defects that typically are not covered in a standard policy include off-record matters, such as claims for adverse possession or a prescriptive easement, or violations of zoning ordinances.

rules.source_text:
Insured Defects
Title insurance typically protects against not only defects that could have been uncovered by a careful title search, such as recorded easements, covenants, mortgages, and tax or judgment liens, but also title defects resulting from forged instruments, undelivered deeds, or deeds executed by a person without legal capacity or authority. Defects that typically are not covered in a standard policy include off-record matters, such as claims for adverse possession or a prescriptive easement, or violations of zoning ordinances.

rules.rule_statement: An owner’s policy protects the owner of the property (i.e., the mortgagor or the mortgagee) and any other person named as an insured from the insured defects. A standard policy also protects an individual who takes the property by inheritance or devise on the death of the insured owner, any estate planning entity (e.g., a living trust) to which the insured owner transferred the property (and any beneficiary who receives the property from such an entity), and a former spouse of the insured owner to whom the property is transferred because of a divorce. However, a standard policy does not protect a subsequent grantee of the property, whether the grantee acquires the property by gift or purchase. The policy can protect the insured from a defect that is not discovered until after the insured has transferred the property to someone else by a warranty deed. Coverage is typically based on the purchase price of the property. A lender’s policy protects the mortgage lender as well as an assignee of the mortgage loan, but the lender’s policy does not protect the mortgagor. The policy typically covers defects that affect the validity, priority, and enforceability of the mortgage. Coverage is based on the amount of the mortgage loan and typically is reduced as the mortgage obligation is reduced.

rules.rule_statement: An insurance policy protects the policy holder, up to the amount of coverage provided, against actual monetary loss due to an insured defect. The policy holder is not required to prove fault, but the holder is required to prove that the defect occurred before the policy took effect. The jurisdictions are split as to whether the policy holder can sue the insurer based on negligence in addition to enforcement of the insurance contract. If the insurer also contracts to provide a title abstract and fails to disclose a defect, the insurer as a title abstractor may be liable for that failure.

Protection

If the insurer also contracts to provide a title abstract and fails to disclose a defect, the insurer as a title abstractor may be liable for that failure.

rules.rule_statement: Breach of the present covenants occurs at the time of conveyance. In most states, the present covenants do not run with the land and cannot be enforced by subsequent grantees. However, some jurisdictions allow a remote grantee to sue the original grantor for breach of covenant against encumbrances. Conversely, breach of a future covenant occurs only upon interference with possession by a third party. Future covenants run with the land and can be enforced by subsequent grantees.
rules.short_rule_statement: Breach of the present covenants occurs at the time of conveyance. In most states, the present covenants do not run with the land and cannot be enforced by subsequent grantees....
rules.source_text:
Time Of Breach
Breach of the present covenants occurs at the time of conveyance. In most states, the present covenants do not run with the land and cannot be enforced by subsequent grantees. However, some jurisdictions allow a remote grantee to sue the original grantor for breach of covenant against encumbrances. Conversely, breach of a future covenant occurs only upon interference with possession by a third party. Future covenants run with the land and can be enforced by subsequent grantees.

rules.rule_statement: A breach of the covenants of seisin and right to convey arises when the grantor is not the owner of the described estate (e.g., another party owns a portion of the property). A breach of the covenant against encumbrances occurs when the property is encumbered by a mortgage, lease, easement, or covenant not specified in the deed. In most states, a breach occurs even if the grantee is aware of the encumbrance. However, some states do not recognize a breach if the grantee had knowledge of the encumbrance, if it was visible, or if it benefited the land. Most states do not find a breach from a violation of a housing code; the states are roughly evenly divided as to whether a violation of a zoning ordinance constitutes a breach. A breach of a future covenant occurs when the grantee has been sued or evicted by someone with a valid superior interest. The grantor is not required to defend against a third party’s wrongful claim or eviction.
rules.short_rule_statement: A breach of the covenants of seisin and right to convey arises when the grantor is not the owner of the described estate (e.g., another party owns a portion of the property). A...
rules.source_text:
Requirements For Breach
A breach of the covenants of seisin and right to convey arises when the grantor is not the owner of the described estate (e.g., another party owns a portion of the property). A breach of the covenant against encumbrances occurs when the property is encumbered by a mortgage, lease, easement, or covenant not specified in the deed. In most states, a breach occurs even if the grantee is aware of the encumbrance. However, some states do not recognize a breach if the grantee had knowledge of the encumbrance, if it was visible, or if it benefited the land. Most states do not find a breach from a violation of a housing code; the states are roughly evenly divided as to whether a violation of a zoning ordinance constitutes a breach. A breach of a future covenant occurs when the grantee has been sued or evicted by someone with a valid superior interest. The grantor is not required to defend against a third party’s wrongful claim or eviction.

rules.rule_statement: A buyer can recover for breach of the covenant against encumbrances the lesser of the difference in value between title with and without the defect, or the cost of removing the encumbrance. To recover for the covenants of quiet enjoyment or warranty, the grantee must notify the grantor of the interference, and the grantor must refuse to defend the title. Recovery is the lesser of the purchase price or the cost of defending the defective title. Recovery for the covenants of seisin, right to convey, or further assurances is the lesser of the purchase price or the cost of perfecting title.
rules.short_rule_statement: A buyer can recover for breach of the covenant against encumbrances the lesser of the difference in value between title with and without the defect, or the cost of removing the...
rules.source_text:
Recovery Of Damages
A buyer can recover for breach of the covenant against encumbrances the lesser of the difference in value between title with and without the defect, or the cost of removing the encumbrance. To recover for the covenants of quiet enjoyment or warranty, the grantee must notify the grantor of the interference, and the grantor must refuse to defend the title. Recovery is the lesser of the purchase price or the cost of defending the defective title. Recovery for the covenants of seisin, right to convey, or further assurances is the lesser of the purchase price or the cost of perfecting title.

rules.rule_statement: When a person who purports to transfer real property that the person does not own subsequently becomes the owner of that property, the after?acquired title doctrine provides that title to the property automatically vests in the transferee. Most often, this doctrine is applied to the grantor of a warranty deed. A related doctrine, estoppel by deed, prevents the grantor from asserting ownership of the after-acquired property. For the interplay of estoppel by deed and the recording acts, see § 3.f.3). Deed Recorded Early, above.

After-Acquired Title
When a person who purports to transfer real property that the person does not own subsequently becomes the owner of that property, the after?acquired title doctrine provides that title to the property automatically vests in the transferee. Most often, this doctrine is applied to the grantor of a warranty deed. A related doctrine, estoppel by deed, prevents the grantor from asserting ownership of the after-acquired property. For the interplay of estoppel by deed and the recording acts, see § 3.f.3). Deed Recorded Early, above.

rules.rule_statement: A special warranty deed contains the same covenants of title as a general warranty deed but only warrants against defects arising during the time the grantor had title.
rules.short_rule_statement: A special warranty deed contains the same covenants of title as a general warranty deed but only warrants against defects arising during the time the grantor had title.
rules.source_text:
Special Warranty Deed
A special warranty deed contains the same covenants of title as a general warranty deed but only warrants against defects arising during the time the grantor had title.

rules.rule_statement: Unlike a warranty deed, a quitclaim deed promises no covenants of title. A common form of quitclaim deed is the tax deed, which government authorities use when selling properties seized for nonpayment of taxes. The grantee in a quitclaim deed (or a grant deed or warranty deed) receives no better title than what the grantor possessed. Recording a quitclaim deed may give the grantee priority over an earlier unrecorded general warranty deed. Restatement (Third) Property: Wills and Other Donative Transfers, § 6.3, cmt. e. See, e.g., N.Y. Judiciary Law § 484; Countrywide Home Loans, Inc. v. Ky. Bar Ass’n, 113 S.W.3d 105, 112 (Ky. 2003). Wash. State Bar Assn. v. Great W. Union Fed. Sav. & Loan Assn., 586 P.2d 870 (Wash. 1978). Perkins v. CTX Mortg. Co., 969 P.2d 93 (Wash. 1999). In re Op. No. 26 of the Comm., 654 A.2d 1344 (N.J. 1995) (if notice is provided, the closing may be conducted by the real estate agent or broker or by an officer of a title insurance company instead of an attorney). Countrywide Home Loans, Inc. v. Ky. Bar Ass’n, 113 S.W.3d 105 (Ky. 2003). Restatement (Third) of Agency § 3.02. Restatement (Third) of Agency § 3.02, cmt. c. Restatement (Third) of Agency § 3.02. Restatement (Third) of Property; Servitudes § 7.14.

rules.canonical_name: Conveyance By Will, Trust, And Operation Of Law
rules.rule_statement: Real property can also be transferred to a trust or conveyed at the grantor’s death by will or by intestacy.
rules.short_rule_statement: Real property can also be transferred to a trust or conveyed at the grantor’s death by will or by intestacy.
rules.source_text:
Conveyance By Will, Trust, And Operation Of Law
Real property can also be transferred to a trust or conveyed at the grantor’s death by will or by intestacy.
topic_path: Conveyance By Will, Trust, And Operation Of Law

rules.canonical_name: Conveyance Of Real Property After Death
rules.rule_statement: A transfer of ownership of real property at death through a will can take effect through a specific devise (e.g., “I leave my residence to my son”) or a residuary clause (e.g., “I leave all other property to my daughter”) when the property is not specifically mentioned in the will. When a decedent does not leave a valid will or the will does not govern the real property, the property is transferred pursuant to the applicable state law of intestate succession. While the intestacy rules vary by state, preference is typically given to the decedent’s immediate family (e.g., spouse, children).

When a decedent does not leave a valid will or the will does not govern the real property, the property is transferred pursuant to the applicable state law of intestate succession. While the intestacy rules vary by state, preference is typically given to the decedent’s immediate family (e.g., spouse, children).

rules.rule_statement: Under the common-law exoneration of liens doctrine, if a testator makes a specific devise of real property that is subject to an encumbrance, such as a mortgage or a lien, then the devisee is entitled to have the land exonerated by payment of the encumbrance from the remaining assets in the testator’s estate. Most states have abolished this doctrine. In such states, the property passes subject to the encumbrance unless the will specifically requires payment of the encumbrance. Crossover Topic: Wills For a discussion of conveyance of real property upon the grantor’s death and the exoneration of liens, see the Themis Wills outline.

rules.rule_statement: A restraint on alienation is a restriction on transferring property.
rules.short_rule_statement: A restraint on alienation is a restriction on transferring property.
rules.source_text:
Restraints On Alienation
A restraint on alienation is a restriction on transferring property.
topic_path: Conveyance By Will, Trust, And Operation Of Law

rules.rule_statement: Direct restraints on alienation are disfavored and are valid only if they are deemed reasonable. To determine reasonableness, courts weigh the utility of the restraint against the harm resulting from its enforcement. If the restraint is unreasonable, then the restraint is rejected, and the property can be alienated. If the restraint is reasonable, then any attempt to alienate the property in violation of the restraint is null and void.
rules.short_rule_statement: Direct restraints on alienation are disfavored and are valid only if they are deemed reasonable. To determine reasonableness, courts weigh the utility of the restraint against...
rules.source_text:
Restraints On Legal Interests
Direct restraints on alienation are disfavored and are valid only if they are deemed reasonable. To determine reasonableness, courts weigh the utility of the restraint against the harm resulting from its enforcement. If the restraint is unreasonable, then the restraint is rejected, and the property can be alienated. If the restraint is reasonable, then any attempt to alienate the property in violation of the restraint is null and void.
topic_path: Conveyance By Will, Trust, And Operation Of Law

rules.rule_statement: A disabling restraint is a total prohibition on the transfer of the property interest by its owner. Such a restraint is always void. A forfeiture restraint effects the loss of property (i.e., the property is forfeited) if the interest owner attempts to transfer his interest. Such a restraint on a future interest or a life estate can be valid. A promissory restraint is a promise by the property interest holder not to transfer the property interest. Such a restraint is enforceable by an injunction against the transferor/promisor to prevent the transfer or by a suit seeking damages from the transferor/promisor for violating the promise. Such a restraint on a life estate can be valid.
rules.short_rule_statement: A disabling restraint is a total prohibition on the transfer of the property interest by its owner. Such a restraint is always void. A forfeiture restraint effects the loss of...
rules.source_text:
Forms Of Restraint
A disabling restraint is a total prohibition on the transfer of the property interest by its owner. Such a restraint is always void. A forfeiture restraint effects the loss of property (i.e., the property is forfeited) if the interest owner attempts to transfer his interest. Such a restraint on a future interest or a life estate can be valid. A promissory restraint is a promise by the property interest holder not to transfer the property interest. Such a restraint is enforceable by an injunction against the transferor/promisor to prevent the transfer or by a suit seeking damages from the transferor/promisor for violating the promise. Such a restraint on a life estate can be valid.
topic_path: Conveyance By Will, Trust, And Operation Of Law

rules.rule_statement: An absolute restraint on alienation of a fee simple is void. In addition, any restraint based on a person’s race, ethnicity, or religion is not enforceable. Further, such discriminatory restraints are considered state actions and are forbidden by the Fourteenth Amendment. Moreover, the federal Fair Housing Act (FHA) prohibits housing discrimination based on race, color, religion, gender, disability, familial status, and national origin. Among the prohibited activities are refusing to rent or sell housing or setting different terms or conditions for the sale or rental of a dwelling on a prohibited basis. There are various exceptions, including an exemption for a residential sale by an owner and a lease of an owner-occupied dwelling with four or fewer units.
rules.short_rule_statement: An absolute restraint on alienation of a fee simple is void. In addition, any restraint based on a person’s race, ethnicity, or religion is not enforceable. Further, such...
rules.source_text:
Prohibited Restraints
An absolute restraint on alienation of a fee simple is void. In addition, any restraint based on a person’s race, ethnicity, or religion is not enforceable. Further, such discriminatory restraints are considered state actions and are forbidden by the Fourteenth Amendment. Moreover, the federal Fair Housing Act (FHA) prohibits housing discrimination based on race, color, religion, gender, disability, familial status, and national origin. Among the prohibited activities are refusing to rent or sell housing or setting different terms or conditions for the sale or rental of a dwelling on a prohibited basis. There are various exceptions, including an exemption for a residential sale by an owner and a lease of an owner-occupied dwelling with four or fewer units.
topic_path: Conveyance By Will, Trust, And Operation Of Law

rules.rule_statement: A restraint that is limited in time and purpose may be valid. In addition, a right of first refusal, which gives the holder of the right the opportunity to purchase the property interest if sold, is usually upheld, as are restrictions on the transfer of a lease (e.g., a prohibition on assignment). Other permissible restraints are those found in commercial transactions when the basis for the restraint is found in the parties’ contractual obligations. Compare restraints on use: Unlike restraints on the transfer of legal property interests, restraints on the use of property (e.g., covenants) are generally permissible. Such indirect restraints on alienation are invalid only if they lack rational justification.

Other permissible restraints are those found in commercial transactions when the basis for the restraint is found in the parties’ contractual obligations. Compare restraints on use: Unlike restraints on the transfer of legal property interests, restraints on the use of property (e.g., covenants) are generally permissible. Such indirect restraints on alienation are invalid only if they lack rational justification.

rules.rule_statement: The owner of an interest in real property may convey or retain that interest as security for the payment of an obligation, usually a loan. The obligation typically takes the form of a promissory note, which establishes the terms of the transaction. If the obligation is not repaid when due, then the holder of the security interest may sell the property and use the proceeds to satisfy the outstanding obligation. The two main forms that a security interest can take are a mortgage and a deed of trust.
rules.short_rule_statement: The owner of an interest in real property may convey or retain that interest as security for the payment of an obligation, usually a loan. The obligation typically takes the...
rules.source_text:
Mortgages And Security Interests
The owner of an interest in real property may convey or retain that interest as security for the payment of an obligation, usually a loan. The obligation typically takes the form of a promissory note, which establishes the terms of the transaction. If the obligation is not repaid when due, then the holder of the security interest may sell the property and use the proceeds to satisfy the outstanding obligation. The two main forms that a security interest can take are a mortgage and a deed of trust.

rules.rule_statement: A mortgage is an interest in real property that serves as security for an obligation. The obligation may be owed by the person who conveys the interest (i.e., the mortgagor) or a third party. The mortgagee is the person with the security interest in the real property, typically a bank. As a conveyance of an interest in real property, the mortgage must satisfy the Statute of Frauds.
rules.short_rule_statement: A mortgage is an interest in real property that serves as security for an obligation. The obligation may be owed by the person who conveys the interest (i.e., the mortgagor) or...
rules.source_text:
Mortgages Generally
A mortgage is an interest in real property that serves as security for an obligation. The obligation may be owed by the person who conveys the interest (i.e., the mortgagor) or a third party. The mortgagee is the person with the security interest in the real property, typically a bank. As a conveyance of an interest in real property, the mortgage must satisfy the Statute of Frauds.

rules.rule_statement: In a majority of the states, the mortgagor (borrower) is treated as the owner of the real property interest, and the mortgagee (lender) is treated as the holder of a lien on that interest. These states are referred to as “lien states.” By contrast, in a minority of states, the mortgagee is treated as the owner of the real property interest, and the mortgagor possesses the right to regain ownership of the real property upon satisfaction of the obligation. These states are referred to as “title states.”
rules.short_rule_statement: In a majority of the states, the mortgagor (borrower) is treated as the owner of the real property interest, and the mortgagee (lender) is treated as the holder of a lien on...
rules.source_text:
Lien Theory Versus Title Theory
In a majority of the states, the mortgagor (borrower) is treated as the owner of the real property interest, and the mortgagee (lender) is treated as the holder of a lien on that interest. These states are referred to as “lien states.” By contrast, in a minority of states, the mortgagee is treated as the owner of the real property interest, and the mortgagor possesses the right to regain ownership of the real property upon satisfaction of the obligation. These states are referred to as “title states.”

rules.rule_statement: In a lien state, a mortgage interest is treated as a lien that does not affect a joint tenancy until foreclosure. In a title state, the joint tenancy is severed upon the granting of a mortgage, and the interest is converted into a tenancy in common. In either case, upon foreclosure, the mortgagee may only foreclose on the undivided tenancy in common interest of the mortgagor, and the interests of other cotenants to the property are not affected.
rules.short_rule_statement: In a lien state, a mortgage interest is treated as a lien that does not affect a joint tenancy until foreclosure. In a title state, the joint tenancy is severed upon the...
rules.source_text:
Effect On Joint Tenancy
In a lien state, a mortgage interest is treated as a lien that does not affect a joint tenancy until foreclosure. In a title state, the joint tenancy is severed upon the granting of a mortgage, and the interest is converted into a tenancy in common. In either case, upon foreclosure, the mortgagee may only foreclose on the undivided tenancy in common interest of the mortgagor, and the interests of other cotenants to the property are not affected.

rules.rule_statement: In some states, a deed of trust (or trust deed) is used in place of a mortgage. The borrower (landowner) delivers title to real property to a third-party trustee (typically the lender’s lawyer) as security for the payment of the note to the beneficiary (lender), with the condition that the trustee reconveys the title to the borrower upon payment of the note. If the borrower fails to pay the note (defaults), then the beneficiary instructs the trustee to sell the land to repay the note. When the property is transferred by trustee in violation of the trust, an equitable lien may be imposed on the transferred property in favor of the lender. For most purposes, a deed of trust is treated the same as a mortgage. However, a primary reason for using a deed of trust instead of a mortgage is that while, upon default, a mortgagee-lender who conducts a nonjudicial foreclosure sale cannot purchase the property at the sale, a beneficiary?lender of a deed of trust can purchase the property at a similar sale held by the trustee.
